Ayahs

قَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١)
Certainly, the faithful have attained salvation (1)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى صَلَاتِهِمْ خَـٰشِعُونَ (٢)
—those who are humble in their prayers, (2)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ ٱللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ (٣)
avoid vain talk, (3)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِلزَّكَوٰةِ فَـٰعِلُونَ (٤)
carry out their [duty of] zakat, (4)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَـٰفِظُونَ (٥)
guard their private parts, (5)
إِلَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ (٦)
(except from their spouses or their slave women, for then they are not blameworthy; (6)
فَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَىٰ وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْعَادُونَ (٧)
but whoever seeks [anything] beyond that—it is they who are transgressors), (7)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ (٨)
and those who keep their trusts and covenants (8)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَوَٰتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ (٩)
and are watchful of their prayers. (9)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْوَٰرِثُونَ (١٠)
It is they who will be the inheritors, (10)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسَ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (١١)
who shall inherit paradise and will remain in it [forever]. (11)

(Commentary)

The Surah contains a call to faith (الإيمان) in Allah and the Last Day, and a distinction between the believers and the disbelievers by mentioning the beautiful qualities of servitude (العبودية) possessed by the former, and the base character and trivial deeds of the latter. This is followed by glad tidings and warnings. The warning includes a mention of the punishment of the Hereafter and the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) — the total divine destruction of a people — that overcame the nations who rejected the true call throughout the course of the prophetic mission, starting from the time of Nuh until the time of the Messiah, ‘Isa son of Maryam (peace be upon him).

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: «قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ» (Certainly, the faithful have attained felicity). Al-Raghib said: al-falh (الفلح) — with a fatha then a sukun — means to cleave or split. It is said: "Iron is cleaved (يفلح) by iron," meaning it is split. al-falah (الفلاح) is success and the attainment of one's desire. This is of two types: worldly and otherworldly. Worldly success is the attainment of those felicities that make earthly life pleasant, namely: survival, wealth, and honor. Otherworldly success consists of four things: survival without end, wealth without poverty, honor without humiliation, and knowledge without ignorance. For this reason, it is said: "There is no life except the life of the Hereafter." (End of summary). Thus, naming the attainment of felicity "success" (فلاح) is based on the notion that it involves "splitting" (شق) through obstacles and uncovering the face of the desired goal.

Faith (الإيمان) — the inner conviction and heart-felt submission to a truth — is the submission to and belief in something while committing to its necessary implications. Thus, faith in Allah, in the parlance of the Quran, is the belief in His oneness, His messengers, the Last Day, and what His messengers brought, accompanied by general adherence. Therefore, we find that whenever the Quran mentions the believers with a beautiful description or a generous reward, it couples faith with righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), such as His saying: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً — "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life" (al-Nahl: 97), and His saying: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ طُوبى لَهُمْ وَحُسْنُ مَآبٍ — "Those who have faith and do righteous deeds—for them is felicity and a good journey’s end" (al-Ra‘d: 29), among many other verses.

Mere belief in something is not considered faith (إيمان) if it lacks commitment to its implications and effects. Faith is knowledge of a thing accompanied by tranquility (السكون) and confidence (الاطمئنان) in it. Tranquility toward a thing is inseparable from commitment to its implications. However, knowledge may sometimes be divorced from tranquility and commitment, as is the case with many who are accustomed to heinous or harmful acts; they admit the heinousness or harm of their actions but do not abandon them, making the excuse of habit. The Exalted has said: وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ — "They impugned them—though they were convinced in their hearts" (al-Naml: 14).

While faith may coexist with disobedience regarding some of its implications due to some psychological distraction (صارف) that turns one away, it does not fail to produce its implications entirely.

His saying, the Exalted: «الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي صَلاتِهِمْ خاشِعُونَ» (those who are humble in their prayers). Humility (الخشوع) — the state of total submission and stillness of the heart before the Divine — is a specific state of being affected that occurs in the one who is overcome before the Overpowering One, such that he is cut off from everything else by turning toward Him. It appears that it is a quality of the heart (القلب), which is then attributed to the limbs or other things by way of extension, as in the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) — according to what is narrated — regarding one who was fidgeting with his beard during prayer: "Had his heart been humble, his limbs would have been humble." And the saying of the Exalted: وَخَشَعَتِ الْأَصْواتُ لِلرَّحْمنِ — "and the voices shall be hushed before the All-beneficent" (Taha: 108).

Humility in this sense encompasses all the meanings with which it has been interpreted in this verse, such as the view of some that it is fear and the stillness of the limbs, or the view of others that it is lowering the gaze, being "lowly of wing" (خفض الجناح), bowing the head, not turning right or left, magnifying the station, focusing all attention, or self-abasement, among others.

This verse, along with the following seven verses, mentions those attributes of the believers that necessitate that the quality of faith be living and active, resulting in its required effects so that the desired goal — success (الفلاح) — may be achieved. Prayer (الصلاة) is a turning from one who possesses nothing but poverty and lowliness toward the courtyard of Grandeur and Majesty, the source of Might and Splendor. It necessitates that the human being who is conscious of this station be affected, becoming immersed in lowliness and humility, and wrenching his heart away from everything that distracts or occupies him from what concerns him and what he faces. If his faith were a sincere faith, it would make his concern a single concern when turning toward his Lord, and his preoccupation with Him would prevent him from turning toward anything else. For what does the purely destitute do when he meets the Rich One whose measure cannot be gauged? Or the lowly one when he faces absolute Might (عزة مطلقة) unmixed with any lowliness or humiliation?

This is the meaning of his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the hadith of Harithah ibn al-Nu‘man narrated in al-Kafi and elsewhere: إن لكل حق حقيقة ولكل صواب نورا — "Indeed, for every truth there is a reality, and for every right thing there is a light." (The hadith).

A Discourse on the Meaning of the Influence of Faith

Religion (الدين) — as has been mentioned repeatedly — is the social way (السنة الاجتماعية) by which man proceeds in his worldly social life. Social ways are related to action, based on a foundation of belief (الاعتقاد) regarding the reality of the universe and man, who is a part of it. From here, we see that social ways differ according to the differences in beliefs regarding these matters.

He who affirms for the universe a Lord from Whom it begins and to Whom it shall return, and for man a lasting life that is not nullified by death or annihilation, will proceed in life in a manner that observes, in the actions performed therein, the felicity of the lasting life and the enjoyment in the eternal Abode of the Hereafter.

He who affirms for it a god or gods who manage affairs through pleasure and wrath without a return to Him, lives a life organized on the basis of seeking proximity to the gods and pleasing them to attain the goods of life and secure the worldly blessings he desires.

He who does not concern himself with the matter of Lordship and does not see an eternal life for man — such as the materialists (الماديين) and those who follow their path — builds the way of life and the established laws governing his society on the basis of enjoying the worldly life limited by death.

Thus, religion is a practical way (سنة عملية) based on belief regarding the matter of the universe and man as a part of it. This belief is not the theoretical knowledge (العلم النظري) related to the universe and man, for theoretical knowledge does not itself entail action, even if action depends upon it. Rather, it is the knowledge of the obligation to proceed according to what this theory necessitates. If you wish, you could say: it is the judgment of the obligation to follow what is theoretically known and to commit to it, which is practical knowledge (العلم العملي), such as our saying: "Man must worship God, the Exalted, and observe in his actions that which will make him felicitous in both this world and the Hereafter."

It is well known that the religious call is related to religion, which is the practical way based on belief. Thus, the faith (الإيمان) to which the call relates is the commitment to what is necessitated by the true belief in Allah, the Glorified, His messengers, the Last Day, and what His messengers brought; and this is a practical knowledge.

Practical knowledges strengthen and weaken according to the strength or weakness of motives (الدواعي). We never perform an action except out of desire for some good or benefit, or fear of some evil or harm. We may see the obligation of an act due to a motive calling toward it, but then be turned away from it by another motive that is stronger and more preferred. For example, one who sees the obligation of eating food to relieve his hunger, but is turned away from it by his knowledge that it is harmful to him and contrary to his health. In reality, the preventing motive, with the knowledge it carries, restricts the absolute nature of the knowledge accompanying the prevented motive. It is as if he says, for example: "Eating to relieve hunger is not absolutely obligatory; rather, it is only obligatory if it is not harmful to the body or contrary to its health."

From this, it becomes clear that faith in Allah only produces its effect of righteous deeds and beautiful psychological qualities — such as fear (الخشية), humility (الخشوع), and sincerity (الإخلاص) — if it is not overcome by false motives and satanic promptings. In other words, if it is not a faith restricted to one state rather than another, as the Exalted said: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَعْبُدُ اللهَ عَلى حَرْفٍ — "And among the people are those who worship Allah on the edge" (al-Hajj: 11).

Thus, the believer is only a believer in the absolute sense if his actions proceed according to the very essence of what his faith necessitates, such as humility in his worship and turning away from vain talk (اللغو), and the like.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ اللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ» (and who avoid vain talk). Vain talk (اللغو) in action is that which has no benefit. It varies according to the matters to which benefit returns; an act may be vain in relation to one matter, yet be useful and productive in relation to another.

Vain talk in actions, from the perspective of religion, refers to permissible (المباحة) acts that do not benefit one in the Hereafter, or in the world in a way that ultimately leads to the Hereafter — such as eating and drinking with the motive of nourishment, which results in the strength to obey and worship Allah. If an act does not benefit one in the Hereafter, nor in a worldly way that leads somehow to the Hereafter, then it is vain talk. From a more precise perspective, it is everything other than the obligatory (الواجبات) and the recommended (المستحبات) actions.

The Glorified did not describe the believers as abandoning vain talk absolutely, for man is subject to stumbling and the slip of sin, and He has pardoned misdeeds if the major sins are avoided, as He said: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُمْ مُدْخَلاً كَرِيماً — "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble abode" (al-Nisa: 31).

Rather, He described them as "avoiding" (الإعراض) vain talk, rather than merely abandoning it. Avoiding implies that there is something in the action that calls for engagement with it, but the person leaves it, turning his face away from it to something else because he does not value it or care for its status. This necessitates the soul's rising above base actions, its elevation beyond preoccupation with what contradicts honor and dignity, and its attachment to great matters and sublime goals.

It is the right of faith to call toward this, for it involves attachment to the courtyard of Grandeur and Majesty, the source of Might, Glory, and Splendor. He who is characterized by it cares only for a felicitous, eternal, and everlasting life. Thus, he occupies himself only with what the Truth (الحق) deems great, and does not deem great that which the lowly and ignorant among people care for: وَإِذا خاطَبَهُمُ الْجاهِلُونَ قالُوا سَلاماً — "and when the ignorant address them, they say, ‘Peace!’" (al-Furqan: 63), and وَإِذا مَرُّوا بِاللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا كِراماً — "and when they pass by vain talk, they pass by nobly" (al-Furqan: 72).

From here, it becomes clear that describing them as avoiding vain talk is an allusion (كناية) to their high ambition and the nobility of their souls.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِلزَّكاةِ فاعِلُونَ» (and who pay the zakat). The mention of zakat (الزكاة) — the prescribed charitable tax or purification of wealth — alongside prayer is an indication that what is meant is financial spending, rather than zakat in the sense of purifying the soul by removing base traits from it. Perhaps what is meant by zakat is the verbal noun (المعنى المصدري), which is the purification of wealth by spending from it, rather than the specific amount taken out of the wealth.

This is because the Surah is Meccan, and the legislation of the zakat well-known in Islam only took place in Medina; thereafter, the word zakat became a proper noun by predominance (علما بالغلبة) for the specific amount taken out of wealth.

By this, the connection of "for the zakat" (للزكاة) to His saying "doers" (فاعلون) is correct. The meaning is: those who are doers of financial spending. However, if what was meant by zakat was the wealth itself that is taken out, its connection to "doers" would not be correct, because the wealth taken out is not an action (فعل) related to a doer. For this reason, some who interpreted zakat in this sense estimated the word "payment" (تأدية), so the estimation for them was: "and those who are doers of the payment of zakat." Also for this reason, some interpreted zakat as the purification of the soul from base traits, to avoid the connection of "for the zakat" to His saying "doers."

In the expression "doers of zakat" (للزكاة فاعلون), rather than saying "payers of zakat" or something similar, there is an indication of their great care for it. It is like someone saying: "I am a drinker" to one who orders him to drink water; but if he wants to express his great care for it, he says: "I am a doer [of it]."

It is the right of faith in Allah to call toward this financial spending, for man does not attain the perfection of his felicity except in a felicitous society where every possessor of a right receives his right. There is no felicity for a society except with the narrowing of the gap between classes in the enjoyment of the advantages of life and the goods of living. Financial spending on the poor and the destitute is among the strongest means by which this goal is achieved.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ» (and who guard their private parts) until the end of the three verses. al-Furuj (الفروج) is the plural of farj (فرج), which — as has been said — refers to that which is shameful to mention of men and women. Guarding the private parts is an allusion to abstaining from sexual intercourse, whether it be adultery (زنا), sodomy, bestiality, or otherwise.

His saying: «إِلَّا عَلى أَزْواجِهِمْ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ» (except from their spouses or what their right hands possess, for then they are not blameworthy) is an exception from the guarding of the private parts. "Spouses" (الأزواج) are lawful wives, and "what their right hands possess" (ما ملكت أيمانهم) are owned slave-girls; for they are not blameworthy in having contact with lawful wives and owned slave-girls.

His saying: «فَمَنِ ابْتَغى وَراءَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادُونَ» (But whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors) is a derivation from the preceding exception and the general rule. That is, if the requirement of faith is to guard the private parts absolutely except from two groups of women — spouses and what their right hands possess — then whoever seeks beyond that, meaning contact with other than these two groups, it is they who are the transgressors (العادون) — those who overstep the limit — that Allah the Exalted has set for them.

We have already presented a discourse on the corruption of the species resulting from adultery in the commentary on His saying: وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الزِّنى إِنَّهُ كانَ فاحِشَةً وَساءَ سَبِيلاً — "Do not approach fornication. It is indeed an indecency and an evil way" (al-Isra: 32) in the thirteenth volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَماناتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ راعُونَ» (and those who keep their trusts and their covenants). Trust (الأمانة) is originally a verbal noun, and sometimes it refers to that which is entrusted, such as wealth and the like; this is what is meant in the verse. Perhaps its plural form is to indicate the various types of trusts that circulate among people. It has been said that trusts generally include every divine obligation (تكليف) with which man is entrusted, as well as his limbs, organs, and faculties with which he is entrusted to use in what pleases Allah, and what people entrust to him of wealth and other things. This is not without some distance from the literal wording, even if it is correct from the perspective of analyzing and generalizing the meaning.

Covenant (العهد), according to religious parlance, is that which one commits to using the formula of a covenant, similar to a vow (النذر) or an oath (اليمين). It is possible that it refers to the absolute obligation directed toward the believer, for Allah the Glorified has called the believer's faith in Him a covenant and a pact (ميثاق) regarding the obligations He has directed toward him, in His saying: أَوَكُلَّما عاهَدُوا عَهْداً نَبَذَهُ فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ — "Is it not that whenever they made a covenant, a group of them would cast it aside?" (al-Baqarah: 100), and His saying: وَلَقَدْ كانُوا عاهَدُوا اللهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ لا يُوَلُّونَ الْأَدْبارَ — "though they had certainly pledged to Allah before that they would not turn their backs [to flee]" (al-Ahzab: 15). Perhaps the intention of this meaning is the reason for the singular form of "covenant," because all obligations are gathered by a single covenant through a single faith.

Keeping (الرعاية) is preservation. It has been said: the origin of al-ra‘y (الرعي) is the preservation of an animal, either by the food that preserves its life or by repelling the enemy from it; then it was used for preservation absolutely. (End). Perhaps the reverse is closer to consideration.

In short, the verse describes the believers as preserving trusts from being betrayed and covenants from being broken. It is the right of faith to call toward this, for in one's faith there is a meaning of stillness, stability, and tranquility. Thus, if one is trusted with a trust deposited with him or a covenant he has made and concluded, he remains stable in it and does not waver through betrayal or breaking it.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلَواتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ» (and who are watchful of their prayers). The pluralizing of "prayers" and the attachment of "watchfulness" (المحافظة) to it is evidence that what is meant is watchfulness over their number. They are watchful so that none of the prescribed prayers escape them, and they monitor them constantly. It is the right of their faith to call them to this.

For this reason, "prayer" was pluralized here, while it was singular in His saying: "humble in their prayer" (فِي صَلاتِهِمْ خاشِعُونَ), because humility is the same in the genus of prayer, so there is no reason to pluralize it.

His saying, the Exalted: «أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْوارِثُونَ الَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ الْفِرْدَوْسَ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ» (It is they who will be the inheritors, who will inherit Paradise, and they will remain in it forever).

al-Firdaws (الفردوس) is the highest of the Gardens. Its meaning and some of its description have already been presented in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: كانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ الْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلاً — "the gardens of Paradise shall be their entertainment" (al-Kahf: 107).

His saying: "who will inherit" (الَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ) etc., is an explanation of His saying: "the inheritors" (الْوارِثُونَ). Their inheriting of Paradise means it remains for them after it was subject to others sharing it with them or possessing it instead of them; but those others passed away from it, so it was transferred to them. It has been mentioned in narrations that for every human being there is a station in Paradise and a station in Hell; if he dies and enters Hell, the people of Paradise inherit his station. This will come to you, Allah willing, in a tradition-based discussion.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: «الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي صَلاتِهِمْ خاشِعُونَ» (those who are humble in their prayers), he said: "It is your lowering your gaze in your prayer and your turning toward it."

I say: It has already been mentioned that this is among the implications of humility, so it is a definition by the implication of the meaning. Similar to it is what was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several compilers of collections from ‘Ali (peace be upon him): "That you do not turn away in your prayer."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Musma‘ ibn ‘Abd al-Malik from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: ما زاد خشوع الجسد على ما في القلب فهو عندنا نفاق — "Whatever humility of the body exceeds what is in the heart is, in our view, hypocrisy."

I say: It is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several compilers of collections from Abu al-Darda from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) something similar in meaning, and its wording is: استعيذوا بالله من خشوع النفاق — "Seek refuge with Allah from the humility of hypocrisy." He was asked: "And what is the humility of hypocrisy?" He said: "That you see the body humble while the heart is not humble."

In al-Majma‘, regarding the verse, it is narrated that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw a man fidgeting with his beard in his prayer and said: أما إنه لو خشع قلبه لخشعت جوارحه — "Had his heart been humble, his limbs would have been humble."

In it, it is narrated: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to raise his gaze to the sky in his prayer; but when the verse was revealed, he bowed his head and cast his gaze toward the ground."

I say: Both are narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from a group of authors of books from him (ص). There are many other narrations regarding the meaning of humility.

In the Irshad of al-Mufid, in a speech by the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): كل قول ليس فيه لله ذكر فهو لغو — "Every speech in which there is no remembrance of Allah is vain talk."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ اللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ» (and who avoid vain talk), it is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: "It is when a man says something false about you, or attributes to you what is not in you—and you turn away from him for the sake of Allah." In another narration, it is singing and idle amusements.

I say: What is in the two narrations of al-Majma‘ is of the nature of mentioning some instances (مصاديق), while what is in the narration of al-Irshad is a generalization through analysis.

In al-Khisal, from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father from his forefathers (peace be upon them), he said: The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: تحل الفروج بثلاثة وجوه : نكاح بميراث ونكاح بلا ميراث ونكاح بملك يمين — "Private parts become lawful through three ways: marriage with inheritance, marriage without inheritance, and marriage by right hand possession."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Ishaq ibn Abi Sarah, he said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about it — meaning temporary marriage (mut‘ah) — and he said to me: حلال فلا تتزوج إلا عفيفة إن الله عز وجل يقول : «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ» فلا تضع فرجك حيث لا تأمن على درهمك — "It is lawful, but do not marry except a chaste woman, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: 'and who guard their private parts'; so do not place your private part where you would not trust [someone] with your dirham."

I say: In this is a generalization of the meaning of guarding the private parts such that it includes abstaining from marrying a non-chaste woman.

The two narrations, as you see, count temporary marriage (mut‘ah) as marriage (نكاح) and wedlock (ازدواج). This is the case in countless narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), and upon this their jurisprudence is built.

The matter is also thus in the parlance of the Quran and during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This is because beyond right hand possession, there are only two types: marriage based on spousehood (زوجية) and adultery (زنا). Allah has forbidden adultery and emphasized its prohibition in many verses in Meccan and Medinan Surahs, such as Surah al-Furqan and al-Isra (both Meccan), and Surah al-Nur and al-Mumtahanah (both Medinan).

Then He called it sifah (سفاح) — fornication or illicit sexual relations — and forbade it in Surah al-Nisa and al-Ma’idah. Then He called it fuhsha (فحشاء) — indecency or gross immorality — and prohibited it and censured it in Surah al-A‘raf, al-‘Ankabut, and Yusuf (which are Meccan), and in Surah al-Nahl, al-Baqarah, and al-Nur (the latter two being Medinan).

Then He called it fahishah (فاحشة) — an abominable act or lewdness — and forbade it in Surah al-A‘raf, al-An‘am, al-Isra, al-Naml, al-‘Ankabut, al-Shura, and al-Najm (which are Meccan), and in Surah al-Nisa, al-Nur, al-Ahzab, and al-Talaq (which are Medinan).

He also forbade it by allusion in the verse of al-Mu’minun: "But whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors," and its like in Surah al-Ma‘arij. It was well-known from the beginning of the mission regarding the matter of Islam that it forbids wine and adultery (1).

If temporary marriage (mut‘ah) were not wedlock and the woman contracted in it were not a spouse (زوج) included in His saying: "except from their spouses," it would be adultery. It is known by necessity that temporary marriage was practiced in Mecca before the Hijrah to some extent, and likewise in Medina after the Hijrah to some extent. The implication of this would be that it was adultery which the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) permitted due to a necessity that required it — if we were to ignore His saying, the Exalted: فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ — "For the enjoyment you have had from them, give them their dowries" (al-Nisa: 24). The implication of that would be that the verse of Surah al-Mu’minun — "except from their spouses or what their right hands possess... the transgressors" — abrogated the previous permission of temporary marriage. Then the permission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or the permission of the verse of Surah al-Nisa would be an abrogation of all the Meccan verses forbidding adultery and some of the Medinan ones revealed before the permission. This is especially so according to the view of those who say that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) permitted it then forbade it time (2) after time; for its implication is the abrogation of the verses forbidding adultery, then their reinforcement, then their abrogation, then their reinforcement several times. No Muslim has ever said that they are abrogated, let alone abrogation after abrogation. Is this anything but playing with the speech of Allah, from which the station of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is far removed?

Furthermore, the verses forbidding adultery, by their context and the reasoning contained therein, are resistant to abrogation. How can it be conceived that Allah, the Glorified, would name an act an indecency (فاحشة), a gross immorality (فحشاء), and an evil way, and inform that whoever does it shall meet the penalty of sin, his punishment being doubled on the Day of Resurrection and he remaining in it forever in humiliation, and then He permits its commission, then forbids it, then permits it?

Moreover, the very principle of abrogating the Quran by a hadith is meaningless (3).

Furthermore, a number of those who engaged in temporary marriage during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) were among the prominent companions, and they were as they were in preserving the outward forms of the laws. How then did they seek permission from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding an indecency? And how did they not find it loathsome? And how did they accept the shame and disgrace, when al-Zubayr contracted a temporary marriage with Asma daughter of Abu Bakr, and she bore him ‘Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr and his brother ‘Urwah ibn al-Zubayr, and they inherited from him after his killing, and they were all among the companions?

Furthermore, the narrations indicating the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prohibition of temporary marriage are contradictory. What they have accepted of the saying of ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab when he forbade temporary marriage during the days of his caliphate, and what has been narrated from him regarding the story, belies these narrations and refutes the report of abrogation. A portion of the discourse on this meaning has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ ما وَراءَ ذلِكُمْ أَنْ تَبْتَغُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ فَمَا اسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِهِ مِنْهُنَّ فَآتُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً — "Beyond that, it is lawful for you to seek [wives] with your wealth, in wedlock, not in license. For the enjoyment you have had from them, give them their dowries as an obligation" (al-Nisa: 24).

Among the subtle indications that temporary marriage is marriage and not license (سفاح) is the coupling of the sentence "For the enjoyment you have had" etc., with His saying immediately preceding it: "in wedlock, not in license."

It has become clear from what we have mentioned that temporary marriage in the Law and in the parlance of the Quran is marriage and spousehood, not adultery and license, whether we say it was later abrogated by the Book or the Sunnah — as is the view of most of the Sunnis — or we do not, as is the view of the Shi‘a following the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

Thus, marriage is divided into two types: permanent marriage, which has its laws regarding number, inheritance, ihsan (إحصان) — the legal state of being married — maintenance, the bed, the waiting period (‘iddah), and so on; and temporary marriage, which is built upon ease, and has from the laws of permanent marriage the exclusivity of the woman to the man, the rights of children, and the waiting period.

By this, the invalidity of what a group of them mentioned — that temporary marriage is not spousehood, and if it were spousehood, its laws regarding number, inheritance, maintenance, ihsan, and so on would apply to it — becomes apparent. This is because spousehood is divided into permanent, which has its laws, and temporary, built upon ease, in which some of those laws apply, as mentioned.

As for the objection that the legislation of wedlock is only for procreation through the permanence of spousehood, and the purpose of temporary marriage is merely the satisfaction of desire by the pouring and spilling of water, so it is license (سفاح) and not marriage—

The response is that seeking offspring is a wisdom (حكمة), not a cause (علة) — the underlying legal reason — upon which the legislation revolves; otherwise, the marriage of the sterile, the post-menopausal, the boy, and the girl would not be permissible.

Furthermore, temporary marriage does not contradict procreation. Evidence of this is ‘Abdullah and ‘Urwah, the two sons of al-Zubayr; she bore them for him from Asma daughter of Abu Bakr through temporary marriage.

Likewise, the objection that temporary marriage makes the woman a plaything (ملعبة) to be played with by the man like a ball passed between polo sticks — mentioned by the author of al-Manar and others—

The response is that this applies first and foremost to the Legislator, for it is necessary that temporary marriage was practiced in the early days of Islam for a period of time; so whatever the Legislator answered with would be our answer.

Secondly, all that is intended by temporary marriage — whether pleasure, satisfaction of desire, procreation, companionship, or otherwise — is shared between the man and the woman. There is no meaning in making her a plaything for him without the reverse, unless one is being obstinate.

There is a completion to this discourse that will come to you in an independent discussion, Allah willing.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Ibn Abi Mulaykah, who said: I asked ‘A’ishah about the temporary marriage of women. She said: "Between me and you is the Book of Allah," and she read: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ إِلَّا عَلى أَزْواجِهِمْ ـ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ» — "so whoever seeks beyond what Allah has wedded him to or what he possesses has transgressed."

I say: Something similar was narrated from al-Qasim ibn Muhammad. It has become clear from what we have presented that the woman in a temporary marriage is a spouse (زوج), and that the verse permits it, contrary to what is in the narration.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: «فَمَنِ ابْتَغى وَراءَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادُونَ» — he said: "Whoever oversteps that."

In it: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلَواتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ» — he said: "Over their times and their limits."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, he said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: «وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلَواتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ» — he said: "It is the obligatory prayer." I said: «الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمُونَ» (those who are constant in their prayer) — he said: "It is the supererogatory prayer."

In al-Majma‘, it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: ما منكم من أحد إلا له منزلان : منزل في الجنة ومنزل في النار ـ فإن مات ودخل النار ورث أهل الجنة منزله — "There is none among you but that he has two stations: a station in Paradise and a station in Hell. If he dies and enters Hell, the people of Paradise inherit his station."

I say: al-Qummi narrated its like in his Tafsir with his chain of narration from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a detailed hadith. Its like was previously mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْحَسْرَةِ إِذْ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ — "Warn them of the Day of Regret, when the matter will be decided" (Maryam: 39) in the previous volume of this book.

(A Legal and Social Discussion)

There is no doubt that what calls man and impels him toward adopting social ways or establishing laws current in human society is his awareness of the needs of life and his seeking to meet them by establishing and acting upon these laws.

Whenever a need is more basic and closer to simple nature, seeking to meet it is more obligatory, and negligence in fulfilling it is more disastrous and harmful. The need for the very basis of nourishment — with which life revolves — is like the need for enjoying various colors of food and types of fruits, and so on.

Among the primary human needs is the need of each of its two types — males and females — for the other through marriage and intimacy. There is no doubt that what is desired from the perspective of creation and origination by this is the survival of the species, and man has been equipped with the instinct of sexual desire to seek that through it.

Therefore, we find the human societies that we observe or hear news of following the way of wedlock and the formation of the household. It has been thus since their most ancient eras; the survival of the species has not been guaranteed except by wedlock.

What we have mentioned is not refuted by the fact that modern civilization has established the way of wedlock on the basis of partnership in life rather than the basis of procreation or satisfying the instinct. For this construction — besides being a modern, non-natural construction — has not yet impelled any of the societies following it to make this life-partnership common between men themselves or women themselves; and this is only because of its contradiction to what human nature impels toward.

In short, wedlock is a natural way (سنة طبيعية) that has never ceased and will never cease to be current in human societies. Nothing competes with this natural way in its course except the act of adultery, which is the strongest obstacle to the formation of households and the bearing of the burden of wedlock and its weights, by diverting the instinct of desire toward it, which necessitates the destruction of the household and the cutting off of the lineage.

Therefore, religious societies or simple nature find it loathsome and count it as a censured indecency, seeking to prevent it by any possible means. Modern civilized societies, even if they have not blocked its path entirely and have not prevented it with that total prevention, nevertheless do not approve of it because of the deep contradiction they see in it to the formation of households, the increase of souls, and the survival of the species. They resort to reducing it with subtle maneuvers, promoting the way of wedlock and calling for the increase of children by establishing prizes, raising ranks, and other such incentives.

However, despite the way of permanent wedlock being a legal way followed in all human societies in the world, and the states' encouragement of it and their maneuvers to weaken the matter of adultery and divert people — especially young men and women — from it, there still exist in all lands, small and large, institutes for this act that destroys the structure of society, whether openly or secretly, according to the various ways current in them.

This is the clearest proof that the way of permanent wedlock does not suffice to meet this vital need of the species, and that humanity is still in need of completing this deficiency, and that it is obligatory upon those who hold the reins of legislation to expand the matter of wedlock.

Therefore, the Legislator of Islam coupled the way of permanent wedlock with the way of temporary wedlock to facilitate the matter. He stipulated conditions for it by which the pitfalls of adultery are removed — such as the mixing of waters, the disorder of lineages and inheritances, the destruction of households, the cutting off of lineage, and the non-attachment of children. These conditions are: the exclusivity of the woman to the man, the waiting period (‘iddah) when they separate, and the attachment of children; then she has what she stipulated upon her husband. It does not involve for the man any of the burden and hardship of permanent wedlock.

By the life of the Truth, it is indeed among the prides of Islam in its easy and tolerant Law, similar to divorce, the plurality of wives, and many of its laws. But what avail the signs and warnings to a people who do not hear? One says: "That I should commit adultery is more beloved to me than that I should contract a temporary marriage or be contracted in one."

(1) According to what Ibn Hisham narrated in the Sirah, and we have cited the narration in a tradition-based discussion in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "Wine and gambling are only..." the verse from Surah al-Ma’idah, vol. 6, p. 146 of this book.

(2) We have cited the narrations indicating that in the tradition-based discussion placed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "For the enjoyment you have had from them, give them their dowries," the verse from al-Nisa: 24, vol. 4, p. 308.

(3) This has been explained in the science of the principles of jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh) most comprehensively.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن سُلَـٰلَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ (١٢)
Certainly We created man from an extract of clay. (12)
ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ نُطْفَةً فِى قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ (١٣)
Then We made him a drop of [seminal] fluid [lodged] in a secure abode. (13)
ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا ٱلنُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا ٱلْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا ٱلْمُضْغَةَ عِظَـٰمًا فَكَسَوْنَا ٱلْعِظَـٰمَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَـٰهُ خَلْقًا ءَاخَرَ ۚ فَتَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقِينَ (١٤)
Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue. Then We created the fleshy tissue as bones. Then We clothed the bones with flesh. Then We produced him as [yet] another creature. So blessed is Allah, the best of creators! (14)
ثُمَّ إِنَّكُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ (١٥)
Then indeed you die after that. (15)
ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ (١٦)
Then you will indeed be raised up on the Day of Resurrection. (16)
وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرَآئِقَ وَمَا كُنَّا عَنِ ٱلْخَلْقِ غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٧)
Certainly We created above you seven levels and We have not been oblivious of the creation. (17)
وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءًۢ بِقَدَرٍ فَأَسْكَنَّـٰهُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰ ذَهَابٍۭ بِهِۦ لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (١٨)
We sent down water from the sky in a measured manner, and We lodged it within the ground, and We are indeed able to take it away. (18)
فَأَنشَأْنَا لَكُم بِهِۦ جَنَّـٰتٍ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَـٰبٍ لَّكُمْ فِيهَا فَوَٰكِهُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (١٩)
Then with it We produced for you gardens of date palms and vines. There are abundant fruits in them for you, and you eat from them, (19)
وَشَجَرَةً تَخْرُجُ مِن طُورِ سَيْنَآءَ تَنۢبُتُ بِٱلدُّهْنِ وَصِبْغٍ لِّلْـَٔاكِلِينَ (٢٠)
and a tree that grows on Mount Sinai, which produces oil and a seasoning for those who eat. (20)
وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ لَعِبْرَةً ۖ نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِى بُطُونِهَا وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ كَثِيرَةٌ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٢١)
There is indeed a moral for you in the cattle: We give you to drink of that which is in their bellies, and you have many uses in them, and you eat some of them, (21)
وَعَلَيْهَا وَعَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ (٢٢)
and you are carried on them and on ships. (22)

(Commentary)

When the Glorified mentioned the success of the believers (المؤمنين) on account of the beautiful attributes they possess, He followed it by explaining their creation and the creation of the blessings He has bestowed upon them. This is coupled with the governance (تدبير) of their affairs—a governance intertwined with creation—so that it may be revealed that He is the Lord (رب) of man and of all things, the One Who must be worshipped alone without any partner.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ طِينٍ (Certainly We created man from an extract of clay [23:12]). It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: "The extract (السلالة) is a noun for that which is drawn out (يسل) from a thing, just as 'sweepings' (الكساحة) is a noun for what is swept." The apparent context suggests that 'man' (الإنسان) refers to the species, thereby including Adam and those besides him. Thus, the creation intended here would be the initial creation by which Adam was created from clay, after which the progeny was made from a drop of fluid (النطفة). The verse and those following it would then carry the same meaning as His saying: وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ (and He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid [32:7-8]).

This is supported by His subsequent saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْناهُ نُطْفَةً (Then We made him a drop of fluid [23:13]). For if 'man' referred only to the son of Adam, and his creation from clay meant the ultimate origin of the fluid (النطفة) back to clay, it would have been more apparent to say: "Then We created him as a drop of fluid," just as it was said: ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً (Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue [23:14]), and so on.

From this, it becomes clear that the view of some—that 'man' refers to the genus of the children of Adam—as well as the view that it refers specifically to Adam (peace be upon him), is not sound.

The linguistic root of creation (الخلق), as has been said, is 'proportioning' (التقدير). It is said: "I created (خلقت) the garment," when you measure it to cut from it a piece of clothing. Thus, the meaning is: "We certainly proportioned man initially from an extract (سلالة) of the parts of the earth mixed with water."

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ جَعَلْناهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرارٍ مَكِينٍ (Then We made him a drop of fluid in a secure repository [23:13]). The drop of fluid (النطفة) refers to a small amount of water, and it is sometimes applied to water in general. The repository (القرار) is a verbal noun used here to mean the 'place of rest' (المقر) for emphasis; it refers to the womb (الرحم) in which the fluid settles. The secure (المكين) means that which is firmly established (المتمكن); the womb is described as such because of its capacity to preserve the fluid from loss or corruption, or because the fluid is settled and firmly established within it.

The meaning is: "Then We made man a drop of fluid in a firmly established resting place, which is the womb, just as We created him initially from an extract of clay"—meaning We changed the method of his creation from the former to the latter.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً (Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass [23:14])—until His saying—فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظامَ لَحْماً (then We clothed the bones with flesh [23:14]). The explanation of the individual terms of this verse was provided in the commentary on verse 5 of Surah al-Hajj in the previous volume of this book. In His saying: فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظامَ لَحْماً (then We clothed the bones with flesh), there is a subtle metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية).

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ (then We produced him as another creation [23:14]). Production (الإنشاء), as mentioned by al-Raghib, is the bringing of a thing into existence and its nurturing (تربيته), just as 'origination' (النشء) and 'emergence' (النشأة) refer to its initiation and development; hence a youth is called 'emerging' (ناشئ).

The context shifted from 'creation' (الخلق) to 'production' (الإنشاء) where He said: ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ (then We produced him as another creation), rather than saying: "Then We created him...", to indicate the occurrence of a novel matter that was neither contained within nor accompanied by the preceding material stages. For although the clinging mass (العلقة), for instance, differs from the drop of fluid (النطفة) in its attributes (أوصاف) and properties (خواص)—such as color, taste, and so on—there exists in the fluid a counterpart for each of these attributes and properties that is of the same genus, even if not identical; such as whiteness in place of redness, both being colors. This is in contrast to what Allah produced finally, which is the human being (الإنسان) who possesses life (حياة), knowledge (علم), and power (قدرة). For that which constitutes the essence of his self (جوهر الذات)—that which we refer to as "I"—has no preceding counterpart of its own kind (سنخه) in the previous stages; namely, the fluid, the clinging mass, the fleshy tissue, and the bones clothed with flesh.

Nor did anything precede in those stages that resembles his properties and attributes, such as life, power, and knowledge. Thus, he is a produced being (منشأ), originated (حادث), and preceded by non-existence (العدم).

The pronoun in أَنْشَأْناهُ (We produced him)—as suggested by the context—refers to the human being created as bones clothed with flesh. It is he who was produced and originated as another creation; meaning he was transformed from a dead, ignorant, and helpless matter into a being possessing life, knowledge, and power. He was matter with its own attributes and properties, then he emerged as something that differs from his predecessor in essence (الذات), attributes (الصفات), and properties (الخواص). He is indeed that previous matter, for it is that which became a human being; yet he is not it, for he does not share in its essence or attributes. Rather, he has a type of union (اتحاد) with it and a connection (تعلق) to it, whereby he employs it for his purposes as a user employs a tool—like a writer using a pen.

This is what is understood from verses such as His saying: وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ (They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we indeed be [re-created] in a new creation?’ Rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully’ [32:10-11]). Thus, the one who is 'taken fully' (المتوفى) and seized at death is the human being, while that which decomposes and is 'lost in the earth' is the body, which is not the human being himself.

The conjunctions in the individual parts of the verse vary between 'then' (ثم) and 'and/so' (الفاء). It has been said regarding the reason for this that what is conjoined with 'then' (ثم) possesses a complete distinctness (بينونة) from that which it is conjoined to, as in His saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْناهُ نُطْفَةً (Then We made him a drop of fluid), ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً (Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass), and ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ (then We produced him as another creation). Conversely, what does not possess such distinctness or distance is conjoined with 'and/so' (الفاء), as in His saying: فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظاماً فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظامَ لَحْماً (Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue. Then We created the fleshy tissue as bones. Then We clothed the bones with flesh).

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَبارَكَ اللهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخالِقِينَ (So blessed is Allah, the best of creators! [23:14]). Al-Raghib said: "The root of 'blessing' (البرك) is the chest of a camel. He said: 'The camel knelt' (برك البعير) when it cast down its chest, and from this, the meaning of 'constancy' (اللزوم) was derived. He said: A reservoir of water is called a 'pool' (بركة) because of the constancy of the water in it. Blessing (البركة) is the permanence of divine goodness (الخير الإلهي) in a thing. The Exalted said: لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ (We would have opened for them blessings from the sky and the earth [7:96]). It is named so because of the permanence of goodness in it, like the permanence of water in a pool. The 'blessed' (المبارك) is that which contains such goodness."

He continued: "Since divine goodness issues from where it is not perceived, and in a manner that cannot be counted or limited, everything in which an unperceived increase is observed is said to be 'blessed' (مبارك) and to contain 'blessing' (بركة)." End quote.

Thus, 'being blessed' (التبارك) on the part of the Exalted is His exclusivity in possessing the abundant goodness (الخير الكثير) that He generously bestows and pours upon His creation. It has been previously mentioned that 'creation' (الخلق) in its root means 'proportioning' (التقدير); thus, all this abundant goodness lies in His proportioning, which is the bringing of things into existence and the composition of their parts such that they are proportionate within themselves and proportionate to what lies beyond them. From this, abundant goodness spreads.

His describing Himself as the 'best of creators' (أحسن الخالقين) indicates that creation is not exclusive to Him. This is so because, as previously mentioned, its meaning is 'proportioning' and the measuring of one thing from another, which is not exclusive to the Exalted. In His speech, creation is attributed to others, such as His saying: وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ (and when you would create from clay the form of a bird [5:110]) and His saying: وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكاً (and you create a lie [29:17]).

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ (Then indeed you will die after that [23:15]). This is an explanation of the completion of divine governance (التدبير الإلهي), and that death is one of the stages that man must traverse in the path of proportioning (التقدير). It is a certainty, as previously mentioned in His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً (Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with good and ill by way of trial [21:35]).

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ (Then you will indeed be raised up on the Day of Resurrection [23:16]). This is the finality of governance; namely, the Resurrection (البعث), which is the last stage in the journey of man. Once he reaches it, he remains there and continues to dwell in it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرائِقَ وَما كُنَّا عَنِ الْخَلْقِ غافِلِينَ (Certainly We have created seven ways above you, and We have not been oblivious of the creation [23:17]). The 'seven ways' (الطرائق السبع) refer—by the context of His saying 'above you' (فَوْقَكُمْ)—to the seven heavens. He has named them 'ways' (طَرائِقَ)—the plural of 'way' (طريقة)—which is a trodden path, because they are the passage for the command descending from His presence to the earth. The Exalted said: يَتَنَزَّلُ الْأَمْرُ بَيْنَهُنَّ (the command gradually descending through them [65:12]), and He said: يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ مِنَ السَّماءِ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ (He directs the command from the heaven to the earth, then it ascends to Him [32:5]). They are also the paths traversed by deeds in their ascent to Allah and by the angels in their descent and ascent, as He said: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ (To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous deeds [35:10]), and He said: وَما نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ (We do not descend except by the command of your Lord [19:64]).

By this, the connection between the end of the verse—وَما كُنَّا عَنِ الْخَلْقِ غافِلِينَ (and We have not been oblivious of the creation)—and its beginning becomes clear. That is: you are not cut off from Us, nor are you beyond Our observation; rather, these seven ways are established between Us and you, traversed by the angelic messengers in descent and ascent, through which Our command descends to you and through which your deeds ascend to Us.

From all this, the weakness in the view of some—that 'ways' (الطرائق) means 'layers' (الطباق) stacked one above the other, derived from 'layering the sole' (طرق النعل) when its layers are placed one over another—becomes apparent. So too the view of others that it means 'flattened' (المبسوطات), from 'flattening iron' (طرق الحديد) when it is flattened with a hammer. Furthermore, the connection between the end of the verse and its beginning according to these two views is not clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً بِقَدَرٍ فَأَسْكَنَّاهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِنَّا عَلى ذَهابٍ بِهِ لَقادِرُونَ (And We sent down water from the sky in a measured amount, and We settled it in the earth, and We are indeed able to take it away [23:18]). 'The sky' (السماء) here refers to the direction of height, for whatever is above you and shades you is a sky. The 'water' descending from it refers to rainwater.

In His saying: بِقَدَرٍ (in a measured amount), there is an indication that what descended did so according to the requirements of perfect divine governance (التدبير الإلهي), which proportions it by a measure that does not exceed what is proportioned by a single drop, nor fall short of it. There is also an allusion to His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ (There is not a thing but its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure [15:21]).

The meaning is: "We sent down water from the direction of height in a measured amount—which is rainwater—and We settled it in the earth—which refers to the stored reserves of water in mountains and plains, from which springs and rivers gush forth and which wells reveal. And We are indeed able to take away this water that We settled in the earth in a manner of taking away that you would not know how to find."

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَنْشَأْنا لَكُمْ بِهِ جَنَّاتٍ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَأَعْنابٍ (Then We produced for you with it gardens of date palms and vines [23:19])—until the end of the verse. The 'production' (إنشاء) of gardens is their initiation and nurturing. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَشَجَرَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ طُورِ سَيْناءَ تَنْبُتُ بِالدُّهْنِ وَصِبْغٍ لِلْآكِلِينَ (and a tree that grows on Mount Sinai, which yields oil and a seasoning for those who eat [23:20]). This is conjoined to 'gardens' (جَنَّاتٍ); meaning: "and We produced for you with it a tree in Mount Sinai." It refers to the olive tree, which is abundant in Mount Sinai. His saying: تَنْبُتُ بِالدُّهْنِ (which yields oil) means it produces a fruit containing oil, which is olive oil; thus it 'grows with oil.' His saying: وَصِبْغٍ لِلْآكِلِينَ (and a seasoning for those who eat) means it grows with a seasoning for those who eat. The 'seasoning' (الصبغ) refers to the condiment (الإدام) used for dipping. The olive tree was specifically mentioned because of its wondrous nature. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْعامِ لَعِبْرَةً نُسْقِيكُمْ مِمَّا فِي بُطُونِها (There is indeed a lesson for you in the cattle: We give you to drink of what is in their bellies [23:21]), etc. The 'lesson' (العبرة) is a proof (الدلالة) by which it is inferred that He, the Exalted, is the Governor (مدبر) of the affairs of His creation, Compassionate toward them, Kind, and Merciful. His 'giving to drink of what is in their bellies' means He has provided them with their milk. The 'many benefits' (المنافع الكثيرة) refer to what they benefit from their wool, hair, fur, hides, and other things; and from them they eat.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَلَيْها وَعَلَى الْفُلْكِ تُحْمَلُونَ (and you are carried on them and on the ships [23:22]). The pronoun in عَلَيْها (on them) refers to the cattle (الأنعام), and being carried on cattle refers to being carried on camels. This is transport on land, which is contrasted with transport at sea, which is being carried on ships (الفلك). Thus, the verse carries the same meaning as His saying: وَحَمَلْناهُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ (and We carried them on land and sea [17:70]). The term 'ships' (الفلك) is the plural of 'ship' (فلكة).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from ‘Ali (peace be upon him) who said: "When the drop of fluid (النطفة) completes four months, an angel is sent to it and breathes the spirit (الروح) into it within the three darknesses. That is His saying: ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ (then We produced him as another creation), meaning the breathing of the spirit into him."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Ibn Faddal, from al-Hasan ibn al-Jahm, who said: I heard Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) saying: Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: "The drop of fluid (النطفة) remains in the womb for forty days, then it becomes a clinging mass (علقة) for forty days, then it becomes a fleshy tissue (مضغة) for forty days. When four months are completed, Allah sends two creating angels who say: 'O Lord, what shall we create, a male or a female?' and they are commanded. Then they say: 'O Lord, wretched or felicitous?' and they are commanded. Then they say: 'O Lord, what is his term, what is his provision, and every detail of his state?'" He enumerated several things from that, and they write the covenant (الميثاق) between his eyes. "When the term is completed, Allah sends an angel to him who gives him a nudge, and he emerges having forgotten the covenant." Al-Hasan ibn al-Jahm asked: "Is it possible for one to supplicate to Allah so that He changes a female into a male or a male into a female?" He replied: "Indeed, Allah does whatever He wills."

I say: This narration is reported from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) through other chains and with similar wording.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَشَجَرَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ طُورِ سَيْناءَ تَنْبُتُ بِالدُّهْنِ وَصِبْغٍ لِلْآكِلِينَ (and a tree that grows on Mount Sinai, which yields oil and a seasoning for those who eat [23:20]), he said: "It is the olive tree, and it is a parable for the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and a parable for the Commander of the Faithful (أمير المؤمنين) عليه‌السلام. The Mount (الطور) is the mountain, and Sinai (سيناء) is the tree."

In al-Majma‘: Regarding تَنْبُتُ بِالدُّهْنِ وَصِبْغٍ لِلْآكِلِينَ (which yields oil and a seasoning for those who eat), it has been narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he said: "Olive oil is from a blessed tree, so use it as a seasoning and use it as an oil."

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٢٣)
Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (23)
فَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُرِيدُ أَن يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَنزَلَ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً مَّا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِىٓ ءَابَآئِنَا ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٢٤)
But the elite of the faithless from among his people said, ‘This is just a human being like you, who seeks to dominate you. Had Allah wished, He would have sent down angels. We never heard of such a thing among our forefathers. (24)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌۢ بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ بِهِۦ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٢٥)
He is just a man possessed by madness. So bear with him for a while.’ (25)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ (٢٦)
He said, ‘My Lord! Help me, for they impugn me.’ (26)
فَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ أَنِ ٱصْنَعِ ٱلْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ ٱلتَّنُّورُ ۙ فَٱسْلُكْ فِيهَا مِن كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَن سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْقَوْلُ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُخَـٰطِبْنِى فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّهُم مُّغْرَقُونَ (٢٧)
So We revealed to him: ‘Build the ark before Our eyes and by Our revelation. When Our edict comes and the oven gushes [a stream of water], bring into it a pair of every kind [of animal], and your family, except those of them against whom the decree has gone beforehand, and do not plead with Me for those who are wrongdoers: they shall indeed be drowned.’ (27)
فَإِذَا ٱسْتَوَيْتَ أَنتَ وَمَن مَّعَكَ عَلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ فَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى نَجَّىٰنَا مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٨)
‘When you, and those who are with you, are settled in the ark, say, ‘‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has delivered us from the wrongdoing lot.’’ (28)
وَقُل رَّبِّ أَنزِلْنِى مُنزَلًا مُّبَارَكًا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْمُنزِلِينَ (٢٩)
And say, ‘‘My Lord! Land me with a blessed landing, for You are the best of those who bring ashore.’’ ’ (29)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ وَإِن كُنَّا لَمُبْتَلِينَ (٣٠)
There are indeed signs in this; and indeed, We have been testing. (30)
ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا ءَاخَرِينَ (٣١)
Then after them We brought forth another generation, (31)
فَأَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٣٢)
and We sent them an apostle from among themselves, saying, ‘Worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (32)
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِلِقَآءِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَتْرَفْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يَأْكُلُ مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ مِنْهُ وَيَشْرَبُ مِمَّا تَشْرَبُونَ (٣٣)
Said the elite of his people, who were faithless and who denied the encounter of the Hereafter and whom We had given affluence in the life of the world: ‘This is just a human being like yourselves: he eats what you eat, and drinks what you drink. (33)
وَلَئِنْ أَطَعْتُم بَشَرًا مِّثْلَكُمْ إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (٣٤)
If you obey a human being like yourselves, you will indeed be losers. (34)
أَيَعِدُكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ إِذَا مِتُّمْ وَكُنتُمْ تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَنَّكُم مُّخْرَجُونَ (٣٥)
Does he promise you that when you have died and become bones and dust you will indeed be raised [from the dead]? (35)
۞ هَيْهَاتَ هَيْهَاتَ لِمَا تُوعَدُونَ (٣٦)
Far-fetched, far-fetched is what you are promised! (36)
إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ (٣٧)
There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and die, and we will not be resurrected. (37)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا وَمَا نَحْنُ لَهُۥ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (٣٨)
He is just a man who has fabricated a lie against Allah, and we will not believe in him.’ (38)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ (٣٩)
He said, ‘My Lord! Help me, for they impugn me.’ (39)
قَالَ عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ لَّيُصْبِحُنَّ نَـٰدِمِينَ (٤٠)
Said He, ‘In a little while they will become regretful.’ (40)
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ بِٱلْحَقِّ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ غُثَآءً ۚ فَبُعْدًا لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤١)
So the Cry seized them justly and We turned them into a scum. So away with the wrongdoing lot! (41)
ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ قُرُونًا ءَاخَرِينَ (٤٢)
Then after them We brought forth other generations. (42)
مَا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ (٤٣)
No nation can advance its time nor can it defer it. (43)
ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا تَتْرَا ۖ كُلَّ مَا جَآءَ أُمَّةً رَّسُولُهَا كَذَّبُوهُ ۚ فَأَتْبَعْنَا بَعْضَهُم بَعْضًا وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ ۚ فَبُعْدًا لِّقَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٤٤)
Then We sent Our apostles successively. Whenever there came to a nation its apostle, they impugned him, so We made them follow one another [to extinction] and We turned them into folktales. So away with the faithless lot! (44)
ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَأَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٤٥)
Then We sent Moses and Aaron, his brother, with Our signs and a manifest authority (45)
إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَالِينَ (٤٦)
to Pharaoh and his elites; but they acted arrogantly and they were a tyrannical lot. (46)
فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ مِثْلِنَا وَقَوْمُهُمَا لَنَا عَـٰبِدُونَ (٤٧)
They said, ‘Shall we believe two humans like ourselves, while their people are our slaves?’ (47)
فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَكَانُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْلَكِينَ (٤٨)
So they impugned the two of them, whereat they were among those who were destroyed. (48)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (٤٩)
Certainly We gave Moses the Book so that they might be guided, (49)
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُۥٓ ءَايَةً وَءَاوَيْنَـٰهُمَآ إِلَىٰ رَبْوَةٍ ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ (٥٠)
and We made the son of Mary and his mother a sign, and sheltered them in a level highland with flowing water. (50)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرُّسُلُ كُلُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا ۖ إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ (٥١)
O apostles! Eat of the good things and act righteously. Indeed I know best what you do. (51)
وَإِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَأَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱتَّقُونِ (٥٢)
Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so be wary of Me. (52)
فَتَقَطَّعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ زُبُرًا ۖ كُلُّ حِزْبٍۭ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ (٥٣)
But they fragmented their religion among themselves, each party boasting about what it had. (53)
فَذَرْهُمْ فِى غَمْرَتِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٥٤)
So leave them in their stupor for a while. (54)

(Commentary)

After enumerating His magnificent favors upon mankind, He follows it in these verses by mentioning the call to the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of His worship through the medium of messengership (الرسالة). He recounts a summary of the call of the messengers from the time of Nuh to ‘Isa ibn Maryam (peace be upon him). He does not explicitly mention any of their names except for Nuh—who was the first to rise for the call of monotheism—and the names of Musa and ‘Isa (peace be upon them), who were among the last of them. He leaves the names of the rest ambiguous, though He explicitly states the continuity of the call and the succession (تواتر) of the messengers, and that the people did not respond except with disbelief in the signs of Allah and ingratitude for His favors.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَى قَوْمِهِ فَقَالَ يَا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مَا لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلَهٍ غَيْرُهُ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ — "Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Will you not then be God-conscious?’" (23:23). It has already been mentioned in the stories of Nuh (peace be upon him) in Surah Hud that he was the first of the "Arch-Messengers" (أولي العزم) — the messengers of great resolve who brought scriptures and divine laws — sent to all of humanity and the one who rose for monotheism and the negation of polytheism (الشرك). Thus, what is meant by "his people" is his nation and the people of his era in general.

His saying: اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مَا لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلَهٍ غَيْرُهُ — "worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him" is a call to the worship (العبادة) — the ultimate submission and devotion to Allah — of Allah and the rejection of worshipping any deities besides Him. Indeed, the idolaters (الوثنيين) only worship others besides Him, such as angels, jinn, and saints, based on the claim of their divinity (ألوهيتهم), meaning their being objects of worship besides Him.

Some exegetes have said: The meaning of "worship Allah" is "worship Him alone," as expressed by His saying in Surah Hud: أَنْ لَا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللَّهَ — "that you should worship none but Allah" (11:2). The omission of the restriction "alone" here is to indicate that this is the only true worship; as for worship accompanied by polytheism, it is not considered worship at all. [End of quote].

However, there is a degree of oversight or distraction in this view, for the idolaters do not worship Allah the Glorified at all. This is based on their premise that worship is a direction from the worshipper toward the worshipped, and Allah the Glorified is too exalted for the direction of any director or the knowledge of any knower to encompass Him. Therefore, they believe the proper way is to seek proximity to the elite of His creation, such as the angels and others, so that they may intercede (يشفعوا) with Him and bring them closer to Him. They view worship as being in exchange for governance (التدبير), and the matter of governance has been delegated to these beings by Him, the Exalted; thus, they are the deities (الآلهة) and lords (الأرباب) worshipped besides Him.

From here it becomes clear that if His worship, the Exalted, were permissible according to them, it would only be permissible to worship Him alone, because they do not doubt that He, the Exalted, is the Lord of lords and the Originator of all. If His worship were valid, only His worship alone would be valid, and the worship of others would not be valid. However, they do not see its validity based on the aforementioned claim they hold.

Thus, his (peace be upon him) saying to his idolatrous people: "worship Allah" carries the meaning of saying: "worship Allah alone," as it appeared in Surah Hud: "that you should worship none but Allah." And his saying: "You have no other god besides Him" carries the meaning of saying: "You have no object of worship other than Him," because there is no lord (رب) other than Him who governs your affairs such that you should worship him out of hope for his mercy or fear of his wrath. His saying, as a derivation from that: أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ — "Will you not then be God-conscious?" means: if you have no lord who governs your affairs besides Him, will you not then fear His punishment for not worshipping Him and for disbelieving in Him?

His saying, the Exalted: فَقَالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ مَا هَذَا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ — "But the elite of the faithless among his people said, ‘This is just a human being like you...’" until His saying: حَتَّى حِينٍ — "...for a while" (23:24-25). The elite (الملأ) of a people are their nobles. Describing them with the phrase "the faithless among his people" is an explanatory description (توضيحي), not a restrictive one (احترازي), since none of the elite of his people believed in him, as evidenced by their saying, as recounted by Allah: وَمَا نَرَاكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَرَاذِلُنَا بَادِيَ الرَّأْيِ — "and we do not see anyone following you except the lowest of us, [who are] simple-minded" (11:27).

The context indicates that the elite were addressing the general public with the content of these two verses to turn their faces away from him, to incite them against him, and to urge them to harm and silence him. What Allah recounts of their sayings in these two verses consists of four or five facets of slander or fallacy (مغالطة) that they fabricated and used as arguments for the invalidity of his call.

The first is their saying: مَا هَذَا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ — "This is just a human being like you, who seeks to distinguish himself over you" (23:24). The gist of it is that he is a human being like you; if he were truthful in what he claims of divine revelation (الوحي) and connection with the Unseen (الغيب), then the like of what he claims would be realized in you as well, since you do not lack anything he possesses in terms of humanity and its requirements. Since it has not been realized, he is a liar. How can there be a perfection within the capacity of a human to attain, yet only one of them attains it, and then he claims it without any witness to testify for him? Thus, nothing remains except that he intends by this claim to distinguish himself over you and to gain leadership among you. This argument actually breaks down into two distinct arguments.

The second is their saying: وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ لَأَنْزَلَ مَلَائِكَةً — "Had Allah wished, He would have sent down angels" (23:24). The gist of it is that if Allah, the Glorified, had wished to call us with a call from the Unseen, He would have chosen for that the angels, who are the ones brought near to Him and the intercessors who serve as links between us and Him; He would have sent them to us, not a human between whom and Him there is no such relation. Furthermore, in their descent and their acknowledgment of the necessity of worshipping Him, the Exalted, alone and the impermissibility of taking them as lords and worshipped deities, there would be a clear sign (آية بينة) of the correctness and truthfulness of the call.

The expression of sending the angels as "sending them down" (إنزالهم) is because their sending is realized through descent. The use of the plural form rather than the singular is perhaps because what is meant by them are the deities taken from among them, and they are many.

The third is their saying: مَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَذَا فِي آبَائِنَا الْأَوَّلِينَ — "We never heard of such a thing among our forefathers" (23:24). The gist of it is that if his call were true, a parallel to it would have occurred in the past of human history. Our forefathers were better than us and more intelligent, yet nothing resembling this call occurred to them or in their eras; therefore, it is nothing but an innovation (بدعة) and a false fabrication.

The fourth is their saying: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ فَتَرَبَّصُوا بِهِ حَتَّى حِينٍ — "He is just a man possessed by madness; so wait on him for a while" (23:25). Madness (الجنة) is either a verbal noun, meaning he has insanity (جنون), or it refers to the jinn (الجن), meaning one of the jinn has possessed him and speaks through his tongue, because he claims what sound reason does not accept and says what only one afflicted in his intellect would say. So, wait and watch him for a time; perhaps he will recover from his state of madness, or he will die and we will be rid of him.

These are fabricated arguments that the elite of his people cast to their masses, or each was mentioned by some of them. Although they are fallacious, dialectical arguments (حجج جدلية), they benefited from them when casting them to the people, turning their faces away from him, inciting them against him, and prolonging their misguidance.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ رَبِّ انْصُرْنِي بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ — "He said, ‘My Lord! Help me, for they impugn me’" (23:26). This is a request from him for victory. The preposition "by" (بـ) in His saying "for they impugn me" (بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ) is for substitution (البدلية), meaning: "Help me in exchange for their denial of me." Or it is for the instrument (الآلة), and accordingly, the meaning is: "Help me by that which they denied me regarding," which is the punishment, for they said: فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ — "then bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful" (11:32). This is supported by Nuh's saying: رَبِّ لَا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ دَيَّارًا — "My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless" (71:26). The verse is separated (فصل) because it is in the sense of an answer to a question.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِ أَنِ اصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا — "So We revealed to him: ‘Build the ark before Our eyes and by Our revelation...’" (23:27) until the end of the verse. This is a derivation from the request for victory.
