The meaning of building the ark "before Our eyes" (بأعيننا) is building it in His sight, which is a metaphor for it being under His supervision and protection. The meaning of the building being "by Our revelation" (بوحينا) is its being by His teaching from the Unseen, stage after stage.

His saying: فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَمْرُنَا وَفَارَ التَّنُّورُ — "Then, when Our edict comes and the oven gushes..." (23:27). What is meant by the "edict" (الأمر)—as has been said—is His decisive judgment between him and his people and His decree (قضاؤه) for them to be drowned. The context testifies that the gushing of the oven with water was the sign (أمارة) of the descent of the punishment upon them. This—namely, the gushing of water from the oven (التنور), which is the place of fire—is a wondrous matter in itself.

His saying: فَاسْلُكْ فِيهَا مِنْ كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ — "shuttle into it a pair of every kind" (23:27). The common recitation is "from every" (مِنْ كُلٍّ) with nunation (تنوين), cutting it off from annexation (إضافة). The implied meaning is "from every species of animal." "Shuttling into it" (السلوك فيها) means entering them into the ark. It appears that "from" (مِنْ) is for the beginning of a limit (ابتداء الغاية), and the meaning is: "Enter into the ark two mates, a male and a female, from every species of animal."

His saying: وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ مِنْهُمْ — "and your family, except those of them against whom the edict has already been passed" (23:27) is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying "a pair" (زَوْجَيْنِ). It has been said that coordinating "your family" with "a pair" spoils the intended meaning because the implied meaning would then return to: "shuttle into it from every species your family." Thus, it is better to imply "shuttle" (فَاسْلُكْ) a second time before "your family" and coordinate it with the first "shuttle." This is refuted by the fact that "from every" (مِنْ كُلٍّ) is in the position of a state (حال) for "a pair" (زَوْجَيْنِ), so it is subordinate to it in rank, as we previously estimated; thus, it does not return a second time to the coordinated noun.

What is meant by "family" (الأهل) are his close relations. It appears they are the members of his household and the believers in him, for He mentioned them in Surah Hud along with the family, while here He mentioned only the family.

What is meant by "those of them against whom the edict has already been passed" is his disbelieving wife—as Nuh (peace be upon him) understood—and she and his son who refused to board the ship and drowned when he sought refuge on a mountain. The "passing of the edict" (سبق القول) is the preordained decree (القضاء المحتوم) of drowning.

His saying: وَلَا تُخَاطِبْنِي فِي الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِنَّهُمْ مُغْرَقُونَ — "and do not plead with Me for the wrongdoers: they shall indeed be drowned" (23:27). The prohibition against addressing Him is a metaphor for a severe prohibition against interceding (الشفاعة) for them. This is evidenced by linking the address to "those who did wrong" and justifying the prohibition with His saying: "they shall indeed be drowned." It is as if it were said: "I prohibit you from the very act of speaking to Me regarding them, let alone interceding for them, for My wrath has encompassed them in a way that no one can repel."

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا اسْتَوَيْتَ أَنْتَ وَمَنْ مَعَكَ عَلَى الْفُلْكَ فَقُلِ — "When you and those who are with you are settled in the ark, say..." (23:28) until the end of the two verses. He taught him to praise Allah after settling in the ark for His saving them from the wrongdoing people. This is a clarification after a clarification that they are inevitably destroyed and drowned. He also taught him to ask Him to save him from the flood and to land him on the earth with a blessed landing, full of abundant and lasting good, for He is the best of those who bring to land.

In His commanding him (peace be upon him) to praise Him and describe Him with beauty is a proof that he is among His sincere servants (عباده المخلصين), for He, the Exalted, is transcendent above what others describe Him with, as He said: سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "Clear is Allah of what they allege [about Him]—[all] except Allah’s chosen servants" (37:159-160).

Allah, the Glorified, sufficed in the story by informing of His judgment of their drowning and that they are inevitably drowned. He did not mention the news of their actual drowning as an allusion (إيماء) that their matter ended such that there was no news of them after that, and to magnify His power, to strike terror through His wrath, and to belittle them and hold their matter in contempt. Thus, the silence in this story regarding their destruction is more eloquent than His saying in the following story: "and We made them [bywords of] tales. So away with the people who do not believe!" for several reasons.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذَلِكَ لَآيَاتٍ وَإِنْ كُنَّا لَمُبْتَلِينَ — "There are indeed signs in that, and indeed We have been testing [them]" (23:30). This is an address at the end of the story to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and a clarification that this call, along with what occurred with it, was an affliction (ابتلاء) — a trial and a test — toward them.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْنَا مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ قَرْنًا آخَرِينَ — "Then We produced after them another generation" (23:31) until the end of the second verse. A generation (القرن) refers to the people of a single era. His saying: أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ — "‘Worship Allah!’" (23:32) is an explanation (تفسير) of the sending of the messenger, similar to explaining an action by its result, as in His saying: تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخَافُوا وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا — "the angels descend upon them: ‘Do not fear, nor be grieved’" (41:30).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِلِقَاءِ الْآخِرَةِ وَأَتْرَفْنَاهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا — "The elite of his people, who were faithless and who denied the encounter of the Hereafter and whom We had given affluence in the life of the world, said..." (23:33). These are their nobles who were deeply immersed in the world and clung to the earth, inciting their masses with this speech against their messenger.

Allah has described them with three attributes: disbelief in Allah by worshipping others besides Him; denial of the encounter of the Hereafter—meaning the encounter of the afterlife, by the context of its contrast with His saying: "in the life of the world"; and because of their disbelief in the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), they were cut off from what lies beyond the world and threw themselves upon it. Then, when they were given affluence in the life of the world and gained mastery over its pleasurable ornaments and beauties, the world attracted them to itself, so they followed their whims and forgot every truth and reality. Therefore, they spoke at times by negating monotheism and messengership, at times by denying the Return, and at times by rejecting the call because it harmed their worldly life and their freedom in following their whims.

At times they said to their masses, pointing to their messenger with a gesture of contempt and belittlement: مَا هَذَا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يَأْكُلُ مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ مِنْهُ وَيَشْرَبُ مِمَّا تَشْرَبُونَ — "‘This is just a human being like you: he eats from what you eat and drinks from what you drink’" (23:33). They intended by this to deny him in his call and his claim of messengership, based on the previous explanation of their argument in the story of Nuh.

In their arguing for his humanity and his equality with the rest of people by his eating and drinking like people—which is a characteristic of animals in general—is a proof that they did not see for man any perfection except the perfection of an animal, nor any virtue except in eating and drinking, nor any happiness except in the ability to expand and indulge in animalistic pleasures, as He, the Exalted, said: أُولَئِكَ كَالْأَنْعَامِ — "They are like cattle" (7:179), and He said: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَمَا تَأْكُلُ الْأَنْعَامُ — "As for the faithless, they enjoy themselves and eat like cattle eat" (47:12).

At times they said: وَلَئِنْ أَطَعْتُمْ بَشَرًا مِثْلَكُمْ إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَخَاسِرُونَ — "‘If you obey a human being like yourselves, you will indeed be losers’" (23:34). This is in the sense of their saying in the previous story: "he seeks to distinguish himself over you." They meant that in following him and obeying him in what he commands you, despite his being a human like you with no merit over you, lies your loss and the invalidity of your happiness in life. For there is no life except the life of the world, and there is no happiness in it except the freedom to enjoy its pleasures; in obeying one who has no merit over you lies your enslavement and the cessation of your freedom, and that is the loss.

At times they said: أَيَعِدُكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ إِذَا مِتُّمْ وَكُنْتُمْ تُرَابًا وَعِظَامًا أَنَّكُمْ مُخْرَجُونَ — "‘Does he promise you that when you have died and become dust and bones you will be brought forth?’" (23:35), meaning resurrected from your graves for reckoning and recompense. هَيْهَاتَ هَيْهَاتَ لِمَا تُوعَدُونَ — "‘Far-fetched, far-fetched is what you are promised!’" (23:36). Hayhata is a word used to express extreme improbability, and its repetition is for emphasis in deeming it far-fetched. إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا الدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا — "‘There is nothing but our life of this world: we live and we die’" (23:37), meaning some of us die in the world and others live in it, and we continue as such. وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ — "‘and we will not be resurrected’" (23:37) for life in another abode beyond the world.

It is possible to interpret their saying: "we live and we die" as referring to reincarnation (التناسخ) — the departure of the soul upon death from one body and its attachment to another human or non-human body — for reincarnation is a common doctrine among idolaters, and they sometimes express it as "birth after birth." However, it does not fit the context of the verses very well.

At times they said: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ افْتَرَى عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِبًا وَمَا نَحْنُ لَهُ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ — "‘He is just a man who has fabricated a lie against Allah, and we will not believe in him’" (23:38). They intended by this to deny his claim of messengership along with what his call contained, having already denied monotheism and the Return before that.

Their meaning in saying "we" (نَحْنُ) refers to themselves and their masses; they included themselves with their masses so that the masses would not suspect them in what they commanded them of disbelief in the messenger. Or it is possible that what was meant was themselves specifically, excluding the masses, and they only informed of their own lack of faith so that the masses would follow their example in it.

These sayings arose from the combination of the attributes with which Allah described them at the beginning of the verses: the denial of monotheism, prophethood, and the Return, and their affluence in the life of the world.

Know that in His saying at the beginning of the verses: وَقَالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِلِقَاءِ الْآخِرَةِ وَأَتْرَفْنَاهُمْ — "The elite of his people, who were faithless and who denied the encounter of the Hereafter and whom We had given affluence..." He placed His saying "of his people" (مِنْ قَوْمِهِ) before "who were faithless" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا), unlike in the previous story where He said: "the elite of the faithless among his people" (الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ). This is because if it had occurred after "who were faithless," it would have disrupted the order of the successive clauses: "were faithless," "and denied," "and whom We had given affluence." If it had occurred after all of them, the separation would have been too long.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ رَبِّ انْصُرْنِي بِمَا كَذَّبُونِ — "He said, ‘My Lord! Help me, for they impugn me’" (23:39). Its explanation has already been given in the previous story.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ لَيُصْبِحُنَّ نَادِمِينَ — "He said, ‘In a little while they will become regretful’" (23:40). This is the response to the messenger's call. Their becoming regretful is a metaphor for the descent of the extirpating punishment (عذاب الاستئصال) upon them. His saying: "In a little while" (عَمَّا قَلِيلٍ), the preposition ‘an (عَنْ) means "after," and the ma (مَا) is for emphasizing the smallness of the duration. The plural pronoun refers to the people. The speech is emphasized with the lam of the oath and the nun of emphasis. The meaning is: "I swear they shall certainly be seized by regret after a short time upon witnessing the descent of the punishment."

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّيْحَةُ بِالْحقِّ فَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ غُثَاءً فَبُعْدًا لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "So the Cry seized them justly, and We made them [bywords of] tales. So away with the wrongdoing people!" (23:41). The preposition "with" (بـ) in "justly" (بِالْحَقِّ) is for accompaniment (المصاحبة), and it is connected to His saying "seized them," meaning the Cry seized them with an overtaking accompanied by truth. Or it is for causality (السببية), and "the Truth" (الحق) is an attribute placed in the position of its omitted described noun; the implied meaning is: "the Cry seized them because of the true command or the true decree," as He said: فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قُضِيَ بِالْحَقِّ — "Then, when Allah’s edict comes, judgment is made with justice" (40:78).

Scum (الغثاء) with a damma on the ghayn—and sometimes the tha is doubled—refers to what a flood carries of dry plants, leaves, and decayed sticks. His saying: فَبُعْدًا لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "So away with the wrongdoing people!" is an expulsion and a curse upon them, or a supplication against them.

The meaning is: "We fulfilled for the messenger what We promised him of their punishment, so the heavenly Cry—which is the punishment—seized them, and We destroyed them and made them like the scum of a flood; so let the wrongdoing people be far removed."

He did not explicitly mention the name of these people whom He produced after the people of Nuh and then destroyed, nor the name of their messenger. It is not far-fetched that they were Thamud, the people of Salih (peace be upon him), for Allah the Glorified has mentioned in their story in several places of His speech that they were after the people of Nuh and that they were destroyed by the Cry.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْنَا مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ قُرُونًا آخَرِينَ مَا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ — "Then We produced after them other generations. No nation can advance its term, nor can they defer it" (23:42-43). The clarification of the content of these two verses has been given repeatedly.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا تَتْرَى كُلَّ مَا جَاءَ أُمَّةً رَسُولُهَا كَذَّبُوهُ — "Then We sent Our messengers in succession. Whenever its messenger came to a nation, they impugned him..." (23:44) until the end of the verse. It is said: they came in succession (تترى), meaning one after another, following each other. From it comes succession (التواتر), which is the following of things one by one and individually. It is narrated from al-Asma‘i: "I followed the news (واترت الخبر) when I made some of it follow others with a short interval between the two reports." [End of quote].

The speech is a continuation of His saying: "Then We produced after them other generations." The word "then" (ثُمَّ) is for a delay in mention (تراخي بحسب الذكر) rather than time. The story is a summary extracted from the stories of the messengers and their nations between the nation of Nuh and the nation that arose after it, and between the nation of Musa.

He, the Exalted, says: Then We produced after that nation destroyed by the Cry, after the nation of Nuh, other generations and nations, and We sent to them Our messengers in succession, following one another. Whenever its messenger sent from it to it came to a nation, they denied him; so We made some of them—meaning some of these nations—follow others—meaning with punishment—and We made them tales (أحاديث), meaning We turned them into stories and reports after they had been entities with traces. So away with a people who do not believe.

The verses indicate that it was from the sunnah (سنة) — the established way or law — of Allah to produce generation after generation and to guide them to the truth by sending messenger after messenger; this is the sunnah of trial and testing. And it was from the sunnah of the generations to deny messenger after messenger. Then, from the sunnah of Allah secondly—which is the sunnah of recompense—was the punishment of the deniers and making some of them follow others.

His saying: وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ — "and We made them [bywords of] tales" is the most eloquent word expressing the divine subjugation (القهر الإلهي) that overwhelms the enemies of truth and the deniers of its call, whereby He erases the entity and effaces the trace, leaving nothing but the report.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَى وَأَخَاهُ هَارُونَ بِآيَاتِنَا وَسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ — "Then We sent Moses and his brother Aaron with Our signs and a manifest authority" (23:45). The signs (الآيات) are the staff, the white hand, and the other signs that Musa showed to Pharaoh and his people. The "manifest authority" (سلطان مبين) is the clear proof (الحجة الواضحة). The interpretation by some of "authority" as the staff is not sound.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَى فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَئِهِ فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا وَكَانُوا قَوْمًا عَالِينَ — "to Pharaoh and his elite, but they acted arrogantly and they were a tyrannical people" (23:46). It is said: He only mentioned the elite of Pharaoh and sufficed with them rather than mentioning his people because they were the nobles who were followed, and the rest of the people were followers who followed them.

What is meant by their being "tyrannical" (عَالِينَ) is that they used to exalt themselves over others and enslave them, just as they exalted themselves over the Children of Israel and enslaved them. Thus, "exaltation in the land" is a metaphor for overbearing behavior toward its people and forcing them into obedience.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقَالُوا أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ مِثْلِنَا وَقَوْمُهُمَا لَنَا عَابِدُونَ — "They said, ‘Shall we believe two humans like ourselves, while their people are our slaves?’" (23:47). What is meant by their being "two humans like ourselves" is the negation that they have any merit over them. By their people being "their slaves," they meant their own merit over the two of them, just as they had merit over their people. If the merit belonged to them over the two of them, then it was obligatory that the two of them should worship them, just as their people worshipped them, not that they should believe in the two of them. As Pharaoh said to Musa: لَئِنِ اتَّخَذْتَ إِلَهًا غَيْرِي لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ الْمَسْجُونِينَ — "‘If you take any god other than me, I will surely make you one of the imprisoned’" (26:29). Then He, the Exalted, concluded the story by mentioning their destruction, saying: فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَكَانُوا مِنَ الْمُهْلَكِينَ — "So they impugned them, and they were among those who were destroyed" (23:48). Then He said: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ — "Certainly We gave Moses the Book so that they might be guided" (23:49). What is meant by "them" are the Children of Israel, because the Torah was only revealed after the destruction of Pharaoh and his elite.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنَا ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُ آيَةً وَآوَيْنَاهُمَا إِلَى رَبْوَةٍ ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ — "And We made the son of Mary and his mother a sign, and We sheltered them in a highland, a place of rest and springs" (23:50). It has already been mentioned that the sign (الآية) is the birth of ‘Isa (peace be upon him) which broke the natural order; since it was a matter subsisting in him and his mother together, they were both counted as a single sign.

"Sheltering" (الإيواء) is from al-awy, and its root is "returning," then it was used for a person's return to his dwelling and residence. "He sheltered him to such a place" means he made it a dwelling for him. A highland (الربوة) is an elevated, level, and spacious place. Springs (المعين) refers to running water.

The meaning is: We made ‘Isa ibn Maryam and his mother Maryam a sign indicating Our Lordship, and We settled them in an elevated, level, and spacious place where there was stability and running water.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الرُّسُلُ كُلُوا مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ وَاعْمَلُوا صَالِحًا إِنِّي بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ — "O messengers! Eat of the good things and act righteously. Indeed I know best what you do" (23:51). This is an address to all the messengers to eat of the good things; what is meant by "eating of them" is seeking sustenance through them by utilizing them, whether by eating or otherwise, which is a common usage.

The context testifies that in His saying: "Eat of the good things" is a bestowal of favor (امتنان) from Him, the Exalted, upon them. Thus, in His saying following it: "and act righteously" is a command to meet the favor with righteous deeds, which is gratitude for the blessing. In His justifying it with His saying: "Indeed I know best what you do" is a warning to them against opposing His command and an incitement to adhere to godwariness (التقوى).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هَذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُونِ — "Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so be God-conscious of Me" (23:52). The explanation of the like of this verse has already been given in Surah al-Anbiya.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَقَطَّعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ زُبُرًا كُلُّ حِزْبٍ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ — "But they fragmented their religion among themselves into sects, each party exulting in what it had" (23:53). In al-Majma‘, it is stated that "fragmenting" (التقطع) and "cutting up" (التقطيع) carry the same meaning. Sects (الزبر) with two dammas is the plural of zabur, which is a book. The speech is a derivation from what preceded it. The meaning is that Allah sent His messengers to them in succession, and all were one community having one Lord who called them to His godwariness, but they did not follow His command and they fragmented their affair among themselves into pieces, making them into books; each party specialized in a book, and each party exults in what they have.

In the recitation of Ibn ‘Amir, it is "sects" (زُبَراً) with a fatha on the ba, which is the plural of zubrah, meaning a group. The meaning is: they split in their affair into groups and parties, each party exulting in what they have; this is more likely.

His saying, the Exalted: فَذَرْهُمْ فِي غَمْرَتِهِمْ حَتَّى حِينٍ — "So leave them in their stupor for a while" (23:54). It is said in al-Mufradat: The stupor (الغمرة) is the mass of water that covers its place, and it was made a metaphor for the ignorance that overwhelms its possessor. [End of quote]. In the verse is a threat of punishment. It has already been pointed out that it is from His sunnah, the Exalted, to recompense with punishment after the denial of the messengership. In the indefiniteness of "a while" (حِينٍ) is an allusion to the coming of the promised punishment suddenly.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Nahj al-Balagha: "O people! Indeed Allah has granted you refuge from Him being unjust to you—but He has not granted you refuge from Him testing you, for He, the most majestic of speakers, has said: إِنَّ فِي ذَلِكَ لَآيَاتٍ وَإِنْ كُنَّا لَمُبْتَلِينَ — 'There are indeed signs in that, and indeed We have been testing [them].'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: فَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ غُثَاءً — "and We made them [bywords of] tales," he said: "The scum (الغثاء) is the dry, decayed matter of the plants of the earth."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِلَى رَبْوَةٍ ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ — "in a highland, a place of rest and springs," he said: "The highland (الربوة) is al-Hirah, and 'a place of rest and springs' is Kufa."

In al-Majma‘: regarding وَآوَيْنَاهُمَا إِلَى رَبْوَةٍ ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ — "and We sheltered them in a highland, a place of rest and springs," it was said: "The Hirah of Kufa and its surrounding lands; the 'place of rest' (القرار) is the Mosque of Kufa, and the 'springs' (المعين) is the Euphrates." Narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them).

I say: It is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn ‘Asakir from Abu Umamah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "That the highland is Damascus of Syria." It is also narrated from Ibn ‘Asakir and others from Murrah al-Bahzi from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "That it is al-Ramlah." All these narrations are not free from weakness.

In al-Majma‘: regarding يَا أَيُّهَا الرُّسُلُ كُلُوا مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ — "O messengers! Eat of the good things," it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Indeed Allah is good and does not accept except what is good—and He commanded the believers with what He commanded the messengers, for He said: يَا أَيُّهَا الرُّسُلُ كُلُوا مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ — 'O messengers! Eat of the good things,' and He said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُلُوا مِنْ طَيِّبَاتِ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ — 'O you who have faith! Eat of the good things We have provided you with' (2:172)."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur by Ahmad, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, and others from Abu Hurayrah from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً — "one community," he said: "Upon one creed (مذهب)."

In the same source, regarding His saying: كُلُّ حِزْبٍ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ — "each party exulting in what it had," he said: "Everyone who has chosen a religion for himself is exulting in it."

Ayahs

أَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّمَا نُمِدُّهُم بِهِۦ مِن مَّالٍ وَبَنِينَ (٥٥)
Do they suppose that whatever aid We provide them in regard to wealth and children [is because] (55)
نُسَارِعُ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ بَل لَّا يَشْعُرُونَ (٥٦)
We are eager to bring them good? No, they are not aware! (56)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ (٥٧)
Indeed those who are apprehensive for the fear of their Lord, (57)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٨)
and believe in the signs of their Lord, (58)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِرَبِّهِمْ لَا يُشْرِكُونَ (٥٩)
and do not ascribe partners to their Lord; (59)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْتُونَ مَآ ءَاتَوا۟ وَّقُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ رَٰجِعُونَ (٦٠)
who give whatever they give while their hearts tremble with awe that they are going to return to their Lord (60)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَهُمْ لَهَا سَـٰبِقُونَ (٦١)
—it is they who are zealous in [performing] good works and take the lead in them. (61)
وَلَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۖ وَلَدَيْنَا كِتَـٰبٌ يَنطِقُ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٦٢)
We task no soul except according to its capacity, and with Us is a book that speaks the truth, and they will not be wronged. (62)
بَلْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فِى غَمْرَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا وَلَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلٌ مِّن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ هُمْ لَهَا عَـٰمِلُونَ (٦٣)
Indeed, their hearts are in a stupor in regard to this, and there are other deeds besides which they perpetrate. (63)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَخَذْنَا مُتْرَفِيهِم بِٱلْعَذَابِ إِذَا هُمْ يَجْـَٔرُونَ (٦٤)
When We seize their affluent ones with punishment, behold, they make entreaties [to Us]. (64)
لَا تَجْـَٔرُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ إِنَّكُم مِّنَّا لَا تُنصَرُونَ (٦٥)
‘Do not make entreaties today! Indeed, you will not receive any help from Us. (65)
قَدْ كَانَتْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ تَنكِصُونَ (٦٦)
Certainly My signs used to be recited to you, but you used to take to your heels, (66)
مُسْتَكْبِرِينَ بِهِۦ سَـٰمِرًا تَهْجُرُونَ (٦٧)
being disdainful of it, talking nonsense in your nightly sessions.’ (67)
أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا۟ ٱلْقَوْلَ أَمْ جَآءَهُم مَّا لَمْ يَأْتِ ءَابَآءَهُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٦٨)
Have they not contemplated the Discourse, or has anything come to them [in it] that did not come to their forefathers? (68)
أَمْ لَمْ يَعْرِفُوا۟ رَسُولَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ (٦٩)
Is it that they do not recognize their apostle, and so they deny him? (69)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌۢ ۚ بَلْ جَآءَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَـٰرِهُونَ (٧٠)
Do they say, ‘There is madness in him’? No, he has brought them the truth, and most of them are averse to the truth. (70)
وَلَوِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلْحَقُّ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ ۚ بَلْ أَتَيْنَـٰهُم بِذِكْرِهِمْ فَهُمْ عَن ذِكْرِهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٧١)
Had the truth followed their desires, the heavens and the earth would have surely fallen apart [along] with those who are in them. Indeed, We have brought them their Reminder, but they are disregardful of their Reminder. (71)
أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ خَرْجًا فَخَرَاجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (٧٢)
Do you ask a recompense from them? Your Lord’s recompense is better, and He is the best of providers. (72)
وَإِنَّكَ لَتَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٧٣)
Indeed you invite them to a straight path, (73)
وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَنِ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ لَنَـٰكِبُونَ (٧٤)
and those who do not believe in the Hereafter surely deviate from the path. (74)
۞ وَلَوْ رَحِمْنَـٰهُمْ وَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِم مِّن ضُرٍّ لَّلَجُّوا۟ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (٧٥)
Should We have mercy upon them and remove their distress, they would surely persist, bewildered in their rebellion. (75)
وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْعَذَابِ فَمَا ٱسْتَكَانُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمْ وَمَا يَتَضَرَّعُونَ (٧٦)
We have already seized them with punishment, yet they did not humble themselves before their Lord, nor will they entreat [Him for mercy] (76)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَابًا ذَا عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ إِذَا هُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ (٧٧)
until We open on them the gate of a severe punishment, whereupon they will be despondent in it. (77)

(Commentary)

The verses are connected to His previous saying: فَذَرْهُمْ فِي غَمْرَتِهِمْ حَتَّى حِينٍ — "So leave them in their abyss for a while" (23:54). For when He followed the stories of the messengers with the disagreement of the people regarding the matter of religion (الدين) and their splitting into factions—each faction rejoicing in what they had—He threatened them with a deferred punishment from which there is no escape and no deliverance. Thus, let them wander in their abyss as they wish, for the punishment will inevitably envelop them.

In these verses, He alerted them that their assumption—that the wealth and children with which Allah has aided them constitute a hastening for them in good things—is an error on their part and ignorance of the reality of the situation. Had that been from the "good," the punishment would not have seized those among them who live in luxury (مترفيهم). Rather, the "hastening in good things" is that which Allah has granted the believers success in performing—namely, righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) and the immense reward and great recompense in this world and the Hereafter that result from them. They hasten toward these deeds, and thus He hastens for them therein.

Therefore, the punishment will inevitably overtake them, the proof is established against them, and they have no excuse to offer—such as a failure to contemplate the Word, or the claim that the call is an unprecedented innovation (بدعاً), or a lack of knowledge of the Messenger, or his being possessed (مجنوناً) and incoherent in speech, or his asking them for a payment (خرجاً). Rather, they are people of obstinacy and persistence (لجاج) who will not believe in the truth until there comes upon them a punishment that cannot be repelled.

His saying, the Exalted: أَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّما نُمِدُّهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ مالٍ وَبَنِينَ نُسارِعُ لَهُمْ فِي الْخَيْراتِ بَلْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — "Do they suppose that the wealth and children We provide them with is [because] We are out to expedite the good things for them? No, they are not aware" (23:55-56). The word نُمِدُّهُمْ (We provide/aid them)—with a damma on the nun—is from imdad (الإمداد). Imdad and madd (المد) carry the same meaning: completing the deficiency of a thing and preserving it from being cut off or exhausted. Al-Raghib said: "Imdad is most often used for that which is liked, while madd is used for that which is disliked." Thus, His saying نُمِدُّهُمْ is of the imdad used for the disliked. "Hastening for them in good things" means the swift outpouring of blessings due to their nobility in His sight; thus, the "good things" according to their assumption are the wealth and children being hastened for them.

The meaning is: Do these people think that what We give them during the period of respite—of wealth and children—are good things We are hastening for them because of Our pleasure with them, Our love for their deeds, or their nobility in Our sight?

No, rather "they are not aware" (لا يَشْعُرُونَ); that is, the matter is contrary to what they think. They are in ignorance of the reality of the matter, which is that this is a granting of respite (إملاء) and a gradual leading to ruin (استدراج) — the process by which Allah grants a sinner successive blessings so that they increase in transgression until they are suddenly seized. We only provide for them so that they may wander blindly in their rebellion, as the Exalted said: سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ — "We will draw them on gradually from whence they do not know. And I will grant them respite; indeed My device is firm" (7:182-183).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ هُمْ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ رَبِّهِمْ مُشْفِقُونَ — "Indeed those who are apprehensive for the fear of their Lord" (23:57), until the end of the five verses. The Exalted explains in these five verses, with the aid of what preceded, that what these disbelievers (الكفار) think—that wealth and children are good things being hastened for them—is an error. These are not among the "good things" at all; rather, they are a gradual leading to ruin and a granting of respite. The "good things" in which one hastens are only those found with the believers in Allah, His messengers, and the Last Day, who are righteous in their deeds.

The Exalted articulated their description, saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ هُمْ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ رَبِّهِمْ مُشْفِقُونَ. Al-Raghib said: "Apprehension (الإشفاق) is a care (عناية) mixed with fear (خوف), because the apprehensive one loves the one for whom he is apprehensive and fears what might befall him." The Exalted said: وَهُمْ مِنَ السَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ — "and they are apprehensive of the Hour" (21:49). When it is made transitive with the preposition min (من), the meaning of fear is more apparent; when it is made transitive with fi (في), the meaning of care is more apparent. He said: إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِي أَهْلِنا مُشْفِقِينَ — "Indeed we used to be apprehensive among our families before" (52:26), and مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْها — "apprehensive of it" (42:18). End quote.

The verse describes them as having taken Allah, the Glorified, as a Lord (رباً) who owns them and manages their affairs. The necessary consequence of this is that salvation and destruction revolve around His pleasure and His wrath. They fear Him regarding a matter they love—which is their salvation and felicity (سعادتهم)—so they are apprehensive out of fear of Him. This is what motivates them to believe in His signs and to worship Him. It has become clear from the preceding meaning that the combination of "fear" (الخشية) and "apprehension" (الإشفاق) in the verse is not a redundant repetition.

Then He said: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "and those who believe in the signs of their Lord" (23:58). These signs are everything that points to Him, the Exalted, in any way. This includes His messengers who carry His message, what they were supported with of Books and otherwise, and the Divine Law (الشريعة) they brought. This is because their apprehension out of fear of Allah motivates them to seek His pleasure and carries them toward responding to what He calls them to, and obeying what He commands them through the path of revelation (الوحي) and messengership (الرسالة).

Then He said: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِرَبِّهِمْ لا يُشْرِكُونَ — "and those who do not ascribe partners to their Lord" (23:59). Faith in His signs is what called them to negate partners in worship (العبادة). For faith in them is faith in the Divine Law that legislated His worship, and in the proofs that pointed to His uniqueness in His Lordship (ربوبيته) and Divinity (ألوهيته).

Furthermore, all the messengers and prophets (peace be upon them) only came from Him. Sending messengers to guide people to the truth (الحق) in which lies their felicity is among the functions of Lordship. Had He a partner, his messengers would have come to you. Among the subtle words of Ali (upon him be the best of peace) is his saying: "Had your Lord a partner, his messengers would have come to you."

Then He said: وَالَّذِينَ يُؤْتُونَ ما آتَوْا وَقُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ أَنَّهُمْ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ راجِعُونَ — "and those who give what they give while their hearts quake with fear that they will return to their Lord" (23:60). Al-Wajal (الوجل) is fear. His saying يُؤْتُونَ ما آتَوْا means they give what they have been given of wealth by spending in the way of Allah. It is also said that the meaning of "giving what they give" is their performing every righteous deed. His saying وَقُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the subject of "they give."

The meaning is: those who spend what they spend, or perform righteous deeds, while their hearts are fearful because they will return to their Lord. That is, the motive for their spending in the way of Allah or their righteous action is their remembrance of their inevitable return to their Lord while in a state of quaking fear of Him.

In the verse is an indication of their faith in the Last Day and their performance of righteous deeds. At that point, their attributes are defined: they are those who believe in Allah alone without any partner, in His messengers, and in the Last Day, and they perform righteous deeds.

Then He said: أُولئِكَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ وَهُمْ لَها سابِقُونَ — "it is they who hasten to good works, and they take the lead in them" (23:61). It appears that the lam in لَها carries the meaning of "to" (إلى), and لَها is connected to "those who take the lead" (سابِقُونَ). The meaning is: those whom We have described are the ones who hasten in the good works of deeds, and they are the ones who precede toward them—that is, they compete in them, because that is the necessary consequence of each of them desiring to take the lead toward them.

Thus, He has explained in the verses that "good things" (الخيرات) are the righteous deeds based on the true belief held by these believers, and they are the ones who hasten in them. The "good things" are not what those disbelievers possess, which they consider, in their estimation, to be a hastening from Allah, the Glorified, for them in good things.

It is said in al-Tafsir al-Kabir: "Regarding His saying أُولئِكَ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ, there are two views: First: That the meaning is they desire acts of obedience with the strongest desire, so they initiate them so that they do not miss their time and so that they do not miss them before death. Second: That they are given various types of benefit and forms of honor prematurely in this world, as He said: فَآتاهُمُ اللهُ ثَوابَ الدُّنْيا وَحُسْنَ ثَوابِ الْآخِرَةِ — 'So Allah gave them the reward of this world and the fair reward of the Hereafter' (3:148), and وَآتَيْناهُ أَجْرَهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — 'and We gave him his reward in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous' (29:27). This is because if these things are hastened for them, they have hastened in attaining them and received them prematurely. This view is a better match for the preceding verse because it affirms for the believers what was negated for the disbelievers." End quote.

I say: That which was negated for the disbelievers in the preceding verse is Allah's hastening for the disbelievers in good things. That which is affirmed for the believers in this verse is the believers' own hastening in good things. The justification for this [second] view is that their hastening in good things is, in a sense, a hastening from Allah, the Glorified. It remains for him to explain the reason for placing their hastening in the verse in the position of His hastening, the Exalted, and substituting it. Some have justified it by saying that the change in style is to hint at their complete worthiness to attain good things through the excellence of their deeds—and this is as you see.

The apparent meaning is that this substitution is only in His saying in the preceding verse: نُسارِعُ لَهُمْ فِي الْخَيْراتِ. The intent is to explain that they think that what We provide them with of wealth and children are good things toward which they are being hastened due to their nobility, while they are disbelievers. However, since that was by a gift from Allah and not by their own power over it, the hastening was attributed to Him, the Exalted, and then negated with a rhetorical question of denial (الاستفهام الإنكاري). Then, what corresponds to it was affirmed according to the original state for the believers.

The sum of this negation and affirmation is that wealth and children are not good things toward which they are being hastened, nor are they themselves hastening toward good things. Rather, righteous deeds and their beautiful effects are the "good things," and the believers are the ones hastening toward them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها وَلَدَيْنا كِتابٌ يَنْطِقُ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — "We do not task any soul beyond its capacity, and with Us is a book that speaks the truth, and they will not be wronged" (23:62). What the context provides is that in the verse is an encouragement and incitement toward the attributes of the believers He mentioned, and a dismissal of what people might imagine that would turn them away from donning the honor of these attributes, from two aspects: First, that donning them is an easy matter within the capacity of souls, and not that difficult or arduous path that those living in luxury find rugged. Second, that Allah does not waste their righteous deed nor forget their immense reward.

Thus, His saying وَلا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها is a negation of any burdensome task (التكليف الحرجي) that is beyond the capacity of souls. As for belief (الاعتقاد), He has established manifest proofs and brilliant signs pointing to the realities of knowledge (حقائق المعارف) that He desires faith in, and He has equipped the human being with that which is capable of perceiving and affirming them—namely, the intellect (العقل). Then He took into account the state of intellects in their variation regarding the strength and weakness of perception; thus, He desired from each what suits the measure of their endurance and power. He did not desire from the commonality what He desires from the elite, nor did He ask the righteous (الأبرار) about what He asked the near ones (المقربين), nor did He drive the oppressed (المستضعفين) as He drove the sincere (المخلصين).

As for action (العمل), He only summoned the human being to that which contains his good in his individual and social worldly life and his felicity in his otherworldly life. It is well known that the good of every species—including the human being—is only in that which completes its life and benefits it in its living, and it is equipped with that which it is capable of performing and doing. That which is of this nature cannot be burdensome or beyond capacity and power.

Therefore, there is no burdensome task in the religion (الدين) of Allah in the sense of a burdensome ruling in His legislation based on a burdensome interest. By this, Allah, the Glorified, bestowed favor upon His servants, comforted their souls, and encouraged them toward the state of the believers He described.

The verse وَلا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها indicates this and more. It indicates the negation of a task based on hardship in its original legislation—such as the legislation of monasticism or seeking nearness by sacrificing children, for example—and the negation of a task that is not burdensome in itself but happens to become burdensome in some of its instances due to a specific circumstance in the case, such as standing in prayer for a sick person who is unable to do so. All of this is negated by the verse, even if the aforementioned favor and encouragement are achieved by negating the first type.

The proof for this in the verse is the attachment of the negation of tasking to the word "soul" (نَفْساً), which is an indefinite noun in the context of negation, implying generality. Accordingly, any soul assumed in any event is not tasked except to its capacity, and no burdensome ruling attaches to it, whether it was burdensome from its origin or became burdensome in a specific case.

It has become clear that in the verse is an endorsement of the degrees of belief according to the levels of intellects, and a removal of hardship (الحرج), whether it is in the origin of the ruling or incidental to it.

His saying: وَلَدَيْنا كِتابٌ يَنْطِقُ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ is an encouragement to them by comforting their souls that their deed will not be lost and their reward will not fail. The "speaking of the Book" refers to its clear expression of what is recorded in it—an expression without ambiguity. This is because their deeds are recorded in a Book that speaks only what is truth (الحق); thus, it is protected from addition, subtraction, and distortion. The reckoning (الحساب) is based on what is recorded in it, as indicated by His saying "speaks" (يَنْطِقُ), and the recompense (الجزاء) is based on what results from the reckoning, as indicated by His saying "and they will not be wronged" (وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ). Thus, they are in security from injustice by the forgetting of their reward, the failure to give it, its reduction, or its alteration, just as they are in security from their deeds not being preserved, or being forgotten after preservation, or being altered in any way.

Al-Razi said in al-Tafsir al-Kabir: "If it is asked: These people to whom that Book is presented either deem lying impossible for Allah, the Exalted, or they deem it possible. If they deem it impossible, they would believe Him in everything He says whether the Book exists or not. If they deem it possible, they would not trust that Book because of their allowing that He, the Glorified, might have written in it the opposite of what occurred. On both counts, there is no benefit in that Book. We say: Allah does what He wills. Furthermore, it is not improbable that this is a benefit for the tasked ones among the angels." End quote.

I say: The answer he gave is based on his methodology of negating purpose from the act of the Exalted and allowing for arbitrary will—Exalted is He far above that. The problem persists in all other affairs of the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) that Allah, the Glorified, has informed us of, such as the gathering (الحشر), the assembly (الجمع), the witnessing of witnesses, the spreading of the books and registers, the Bridge (الصراط), the Balance (الميزان), and the Reckoning.

The answer to all of that is: He, the Exalted, has modeled for us what occurs to the human being on the Day of Resurrection in the form of a judicial process and a decisive judgment. A judicial process, insofar as it is a judicial process, cannot dispense with reliance upon proofs and evidences—such as books, witnesses, and signs—and the bringing together of the disputing parties; it can never be completed without that.

Yes, if we were to look past that, it would be the manifestation of the human being's deeds to him in the stages of his return to Allah, the Glorified, by His permission. So understand this.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ مِنْ هذا وَلَهُمْ أَعْمالٌ مِنْ دُونِ ذلِكَ هُمْ لَها عامِلُونَ — "Rather their hearts are in an abyss [of ignorance] as to this, and they have [other] deeds besides that, which they are doing" (23:63). What is appropriate to the context of the verses is that "this" (هذا) refers to what the previous verses described of the state of the believers and their hastening in good things. It is also possible that it refers to the Quran, as supported by His saying afterward: قَدْ كانَتْ آياتِي تُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ — "My signs used to be recited to you" (23:66). The "abyss" (الغمرة) is intense heedlessness or intense ignorance that has overwhelmed them. His saying وَلَهُمْ أَعْمالٌ مِنْ دُونِ ذلِكَ etc., means they have deeds other than what We described of the state of the believers, which is a metaphor for the fact that they have a preoccupation that distracts them from these good things and righteous deeds—namely, the wicked and evil deeds which they are doing.

The meaning is: Rather, the disbelievers are in intense heedlessness or intense ignorance regarding this which We described the believers with, and they have wicked, evil deeds besides that, which they are doing in their preoccupation and hindrance.

His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا أَخَذْنا مُتْرَفِيهِمْ بِالْعَذابِ إِذا هُمْ يَجْأَرُونَ — "until when We seize their elite with punishment, behold, they make entreaty" (23:64). Al-Ju'ar (الجؤار)—with a damma on the jim—is the sound of wild animals like gazelles and the like when terrified. It is used as a metaphor for their raising their voices in seeking help and supplication. It is also said that it refers to their clamor and anguish, and the following verses support the first meaning.

He made their "elite" (مترفيهم) the object of the punishment because the discourse is about those He mentioned before in His saying: أَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّما نُمِدُّهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ مالٍ وَبَنِينَ — "Do they suppose that the wealth and children We provide them with..." (23:55), and they are the leaders living in luxury among them, while others are followers of them.

His saying, the Exalted: لا تَجْأَرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّكُمْ مِنَّا لا تُنْصَرُونَ — "Do not make entreaty today! Indeed you will not be helped by Us" (23:65). The shift from the third person (غيبة) to the second person (خطاب) is to intensify the rebuke and reprimand, and to cut off their hope for salvation through seeking help. What hope or expectation could they have in it when the reports from the intermediaries that they will not be helped through supplication or intercession (الشفاعة) do not cut off their hope for victory as much as the report from the One in Whose hand is victory itself?

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ كانَتْ آياتِي تُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ — "My signs used to be recited to you" (23:66), until His saying تَهْجُرُونَ — "talking nonsense" (23:67). Al-Nukus (النكوص) is to retreat backward. Al-Samir (السامر) is from samar (السمر), which is conversing at night. It is said that al-samir, like al-hadir, is used for both singular and plural. It has been recited as summaran (سمراً)—with a damma on the sin and a shadda on the mim—the plural of samir, which is more likely. It has also been recited as summaran (سماراً)—with a damma and shadda. Al-Hajr (الهجر) is delirium or talking nonsense.

The separation in His saying قَدْ كانَتْ آياتِي etc., is because it is in the position of providing a reason (التعليل). The meaning is: You will not be helped by Us because My signs used to be recited and read to you, but you used to turn away from them and retreat on your heels backward, being arrogant in your retreat, conversing about its matter in the night, talking nonsense and deliriously. It is said that the pronoun in بِهِ (in it/him) refers to the House (the Kaaba) or the Sanctuary (الحرم), but this is as you see.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا الْقَوْلَ أَمْ جاءَهُمْ ما لَمْ يَأْتِ آباءَهُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ — "Have they not pondered the Word, or has there come to them what did not come to their forefathers?" (23:68). This is the beginning of cutting off their excuses for turning away from the Quran revealed for their guidance and their failure to respond to the true call established by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

Thus, His saying أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا الْقَوْلَ contains a question for denial. The lam in "the Word" (الْقَوْلَ) is for specific reference, meaning the Quran recited to them. The speech is branched from what preceded it—that they are in heedlessness of it and a preoccupation that distracts them from it. The meaning is: Is it that while they were in that state, they did not ponder this Word recited to them so that they might know it is the truth from Allah and thus believe in it?

His saying أَمْ جاءَهُمْ ما لَمْ يَأْتِ آباءَهُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ — the word am (أَمْ) here and in what follows is disjunctive, carrying the meaning of "rather" (إضراب). The meaning is: Rather, has something come to them that did not come to their early forefathers, such that it would be an innovation (بدعاً) to be denied and guarded against?

The fact that a thing is an unprecedented innovation not known to predecessors—even if it does not necessarily imply it is false and not true in a universal sense—yet because the Divine message is for the purpose of guidance, if it were true, it would be mandatory for everyone. Thus, if it had not come to the predecessors, that would be a decisive proof of its falsehood.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ لَمْ يَعْرِفُوا رَسُولَهُمْ فَهُمْ لَهُ مُنْكِرُونَ — "Or did they not recognize their Apostle, that they should deny him?" (23:69). What is meant by "recognizing the Messenger" is knowing him by his lineage and status, and in short, by his spiritual traits and psychological faculties—both acquired and inherited—until it becomes clear through them that he is truthful in what he says, a believer in what he calls to, and supported by Allah. They had known from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) his previous state before the mission (البعثة). He was an orphan who had lost both parents, who did not read or write, and did not receive discipline from a tutor or upbringing from a mentor. Then, they did not find in him anything that an intellect would find ugly, or a nature would deny, or an opinion would find loathsome; nor did they find a desire for kingship, a greed for wealth, or a craving for status. He remained as he was for years of his life, and then suddenly he calls to success and felicity, summons to realities and knowledge that dazzle the intellects, calls to a Divine Law that bewilders the minds, and recites a Book.

So they have recognized their Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by his specific attributes that are miraculous for others. Had they not known him, it would have been an excuse for them in their turning away from his religion and their disdain for believing in him; because the meaning of not knowing him in this way would be finding him with other than some of these attributes or not establishing them in him. It is well known that handing over the reins to one of such a state is something the intellect does not permit.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ بَلْ جاءَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كارِهُونَ — "Or do they say, ‘There is madness in him’? Rather, he has brought them the truth, and most of them are averse to the truth" (23:70). This is another excuse of theirs that they clung to when they said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ — "O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, indeed you are crazy" (15:6). He mentioned it and refuted it by the necessary consequence of His saying: بَلْ جاءَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ.

The implication of His saying بَلْ جاءَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كارِهُونَ is a transition (إضراب) from a deleted sentence, the estimation of which is: they are liars in their saying "there is madness in him" and in their excusing their lack of faith in him by that. Rather, they only hated believing in him because he brought the truth, and most of them are averse to the truth.

Its necessary consequence is the refutation of their saying with a proof hinted at by this transition, which is: their saying "there is madness in him," if it were true, his speech would be disordered in its arrangement, inconsistent in its meaning, and defective, just as his intellect would be defective, not aiming at a correct goal. But his speech is not like that; it calls only to truth and brings only truth. Where is that from the speech of a madman who does not know what he wants and is not aware of what he says?

He only attributed the aversion to "most of them" because among them are the oppressed (مستضعفين) who are not taken into account whether they desired or were averse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوِ اتَّبَعَ الْحَقُّ أَهْواءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيهِنَّ بَلْ أَتَيْناهُمْ بِذِكْرِهِمْ فَهُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِهِمْ مُعْرِضُونَ — "Had the truth followed their desires, the heavens and the earth would have surely fallen apart [along] with whoever is in them. Rather, We have brought them their Reminder, but they are disregardful of their Reminder" (23:71). Since He mentioned that most of them are averse to the truth, and they only hate the truth because of its opposition to their whims (أهواءهم), they want the truth—that is, the true call—to follow their whims, and this is something that can never be.

For if the truth followed their whims, and they were left to what they desired of belief and action—so they worshipped idols, took lords, denied the messengership and the Return (المعاد), and committed what they wanted of indecency, evil, and corruption—it would be permissible for the truth to follow them in other parts of creation and the system (النظام) that runs within it by the truth, since there is no difference between one truth and another. Thus, each of them would be given what he craves of the running of the system, and in that would be the corruption of the heavens and the earth and whoever is in them, the disorder of the system, and the violation of the universal laws (القوانين الكلية) running in the cosmos (الكون); for it is clear that whim does not stop at a limit nor settle upon a foundation.

In another, more precise explanation more in accord with what the Quran gives of the reality of the upright religion (الدين القيم): the human being is a cosmic reality (حقيقة كونية) linked in his existence to the general cosmos. He has, in his species-nature, a goal which is his felicity, and a path has been drawn for him toward his felicity and perfection which he attains by traversing the path established for it, similar to other existing species. The general cosmos and his own specific creation have equipped him with powers and instruments that suit his felicity and the path established for it—namely, the belief and action that lead him to his felicity.

Thus, the path that leads the human being to his felicity—meaning the specific beliefs and actions mediating between him and his felicity, which are called "religion" and the "way of life" (سنة الحياة)—is determined according to the requirement of the general cosmic system and the specific human system which we call the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and is following that.

This is what He, the Exalted, points to in His saying: فأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion" (30:30).

So the way of life that leads its traveler to human felicity is a determined path required by the system through the truth, and revealed by the equipments of his existence through the truth. This truth consists of the fixed, unchanging laws that govern the cosmic system—of which the human system is one part—and manage it and drive it toward its goals; it is that which Allah, the Glorified, has decreed, so it was an inevitable decree.

If the truth followed their whims, and thus required for them a Divine Law based on the arbitrariness of their whims, that would only be possible by the changing of the parts of the cosmos from what they are, the replacement of causes and effects with others, and the changing of the ordered links into arbitrary, disordered, and conflicting links whose requirements would match the arbitrariness of their whims. In that would be the corruption of the heavens and the earth and whoever is in them, in their essences and the management running within them, because their being and their management are mixed and indistinguishable, and creation (الخلق) and command (الأمر) are connected and inseparable.

This is what His saying points to: وَلَوِ اتَّبَعَ الْحَقُّ أَهْواءَهُمْ لَفَسَدَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيهِنَّ.

His saying: بَلْ أَتَيْناهُمْ بِذِكْرِهِمْ فَهُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِهِمْ مُعْرِضُونَ. There is no doubt that what is meant by "the Reminder" (الذكر) is the Quran, as He said: وَهذا ذِكْرٌ مُبارَكٌ — "And this is a blessed Reminder" (21:50), and He said: وَإِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ — "and it is indeed a reminder for you and your people" (43:44), among other verses. Perhaps expressing it as "the Reminder" after His saying أَمْ يَقُولُونَ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ is a kind of counter-response to their saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ — "O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, indeed you are crazy" (15:6).

In any case, it is called a "Reminder" because it reminds them of Allah or mentions to them the religion of Allah consisting of true belief and righteous action. The second is more in accord with the beginning of the verse according to its preceding meaning. It was attributed to them (بِذِكْرِهِمْ) because the religion—meaning the true call—varies with respect to people in terms of brevity and detail, and what the Quran mentions is the final stage of detail, as its Divine Law is the last of the Laws.

The meaning is: The truth did not follow their whims; rather, We brought them a Book that reminds them of—or they are mentioned by—their religion which is specific to them, and it follows from this that they are turning away from their own specific religion.

Many of them said that the attribution of the "Reminder" to them is for honor (التشريف), similar to His saying: وَإِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ وَسَوْفَ تُسْئَلُونَ — "and it is indeed a reminder for you and your people, and you will be questioned" (43:44). The meaning would be: Rather, We brought them their pride and their honor which they were obligated to accept with complete receptivity, yet they, by what they did of retreating from their own pride and honor, are turning away.

In this is the point that there is no doubt that the Noble Quran is an honor for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) since it was revealed to him, and for the people of his house (أهل بيته) since it was revealed in their house, and for the Arabs since it was revealed in their language, and for the nation (الأمة) since it was revealed for their guidance. However, the attribution in the verse is not for this consideration, but for the consideration of the specificity of this religion to this nation, which is more in accord with the beginning of the verse in the sense previously indicated.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ خَرْجاً فَخَراجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَهو خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ — "Do you ask them for a tribute? But the tribute of your Lord is better, and He is the best of providers" (23:72). It is said in Majma' al-Bayan: "The origin of kharaj (الخراج) and kharj (الخرج) is one, which is the yield that is produced as a regular duty." End quote.

This is the fourth of the excuses mentioned in these verses, which were refuted and for which they were rebuked. Allah mentioned it by His saying أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ خَرْجاً, meaning wealth they would pay to you as a custom or duty. Then He mentioned the self-sufficiency of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by His saying فَخَراجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ, meaning that Allah is your provider and you have no need for their tribute. The command to inform them of this has been repeated in the verses: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any wage for it’" (6:90, 42:23).

With what was mentioned in the verse, four of the refuted excuses have been completed, and they are varied: the first, أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا الْقَوْلَ, refers to the Quran; the second, أَمْ جاءَهُمْ ما لَمْ يَأْتِ آباءَهُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ, to the religion to which the call is made; the third, أَمْ يَقُولُونَ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ, to the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) himself; and the fourth, أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ خَرْجاً, to his conduct (سيرته).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّكَ لَتَدْعُوهُمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ عَنِ الصِّراطِ لَناكِبُونَ — "Indeed you invite them to a straight path, and indeed those who do not believe in the Hereafter deviate from the path" (23:73-74). Al-Nakb (النكب) and al-nukub (النكوب) mean deviating from the road and swerving from a thing.

It has preceded in the commentary of Surah al-Fatihah that the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — is the clear road that does not vary and does not fail in its ruling, which is its delivering its travelers to the intended goal. This is the attribute of the truth (الحق), for the truth is one; its parts do not vary by contradiction or conflict, and it does not fail in its objective to which it guides. Thus, the truth is a straight path. Since He mentioned that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) guides to the truth, its necessary consequence was what He mentioned: that he guides to a straight path.

Furthermore, since those who disbelieved were averse to the truth as He mentioned, they are deviating from the path—meaning the straight path—swerving toward something else.

He only cited among their attributes their lack of faith in the Hereafter (الآخرة) and restricted it to that because the religion of truth is built on the foundation that for the human being there is an eternal life that is not nullified by death, and in it he has a felicity that must be acquired through true belief and righteous action, and a wretchedness that must be avoided. These people, by their denial of the afterlife, deviate from the truth and the straight path.

In another explanation: the religion of truth is a collection of beliefs and practical duties (تكاليف), and a duty is not completed except with a reckoning and a recompense, and the Day of Resurrection has been appointed for that. Since these people do not believe in the Hereafter, religion becomes futile to them; they see nothing of life except the material worldly life, and no felicity remains for them except attaining material pleasures—which is the enjoyment of the belly and what is below it. The necessary consequence of this is that the whim (الهوى) becomes the followed guide, whether it agrees with the truth or opposes it.

The sum of the two verses is that they are not believers in you because you call to a straight path, while they have no concern except deviating and swerving from it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ رَحِمْناهُمْ وَكَشَفْنا ما بِهِمْ مِنْ ضُرٍّ — "Were We to have mercy on them and remove their distress" (23:75), until His saying وَما يَتَضَرَّعُونَ — "nor do they entreat [Him]" (23:76). Al-Lajaj (اللجاج) is persistence and obstinacy in engaging in a forbidden act. Al-'Amah (العمه) is wavering in a matter out of bewilderment. Al-Raghib mentioned both. In al-Majma': al-istikanah (الاستكانة) is humility (الخضوع); it is the istaf'ala form from kawn (being), and the meaning is they did not seek to be in a state of humility. End quote.

His saying وَلَوْ رَحِمْناهُمْ is an explanation and support for their deviation from the path: that if We were to have mercy on them and remove their distress, they would not return by meeting that with gratitude; rather, they would persist in their rebellion against the truth and continue wavering in their transgression. Thus, mercy by removing distress does not benefit them, just as frightening them with punishment and vengeance does not benefit them. For We have already seized them with punishment, yet they did not humble themselves to their Lord, nor do they entreat Him. These people are not benefited by, nor do they mount, the path of truth—neither by mercy through removing distress, nor by vengeance and frightening through seizing with punishment.

What is meant by "punishment" is the light punishment that does not cut the human being off from general causes, by the evidence of what is in the following verse. Thus, the objection—that returning to Allah at the time of desperation and being cut off from causes is among the instincts (غريزيات) of the human being, as repeatedly mentioned in the Quran—is not valid. How could it be possible that the punishment seizes them and then they do not humble themselves nor entreat?

His saying in the first verse ما بِهِمْ مِنْ ضُرٍّ (their distress) and in the second وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْناهُمْ بِالْعَذابِ (We have already seized them with punishment) indicates that the speech is looking at a punishment that has occurred and has not yet been lifted at the time of the verses' revelation. It is probable that it was the drought with which the people of Mecca were afflicted, and mention of it has appeared in the narrations.

His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا فَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ باباً ذا عَذابٍ شَدِيدٍ إِذا هُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ — "until when We open for them a door to a severe punishment, behold, they are despondent in it" (23:77). That is, they remain in this state of theirs—neither mercy nor punishment benefiting them—until We open for them a door of severe punishment, which is death with the punishment of the Hereafter that follows it—according to what the context of the verses, especially the coming verses, provides—and then despondency (الإبلاس) and despair of all good suddenly overtake them.

He has concluded this section of the speech—meaning His saying أَفَلَمْ يَدَّبَّرُوا الْقَوْلَ etc.—with something similar to how He concluded the previous section—meaning His saying أَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّما نُمِدُّهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ مالٍ وَبَنِينَ until the end of the verses—which is the mention of the punishment of the Hereafter, and He will return to it again.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ هُمْ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ رَبِّهِمْ مُشْفِقُونَ until His saying يُؤْتُونَ ما آتَوْا, he said: "Of worship and obedience."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Faryabi, Ahmad, 'Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Majah, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in Na't al-Kha'ifin, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated from Aisha, who said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, the saying of Allah: وَالَّذِينَ يُؤْتُونَ ما آتَوْا وَقُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ, is it the man who commits adultery, steals, and drinks wine—while he, despite that, fears Allah?' He said: 'No, but it is the man who fasts, gives charity, and prays—while he, despite that, fears Allah that it might not be accepted from him.'"

In al-Majma', regarding His saying وَقُلُوبُهُمْ وَجِلَةٌ, Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Its meaning is: fearing that it will not be accepted from them." In another narration: "He performs it while he is fearful and hopeful."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: 'Abd al-Razzaq, 'Abd ibn Humayd, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Qatadah regarding حَتَّى إِذا أَخَذْنا مُتْرَفِيهِمْ بِالْعَذابِ: "He said: It was mentioned to us that it was revealed concerning those whom Allah killed on the day of Badr."

I say: Something similar was narrated from al-Nasa'i from Ibn Abbas, and his wording was: "He said: They are the people of Badr." However, the context of the verses does not align with the content of these two narrations.

In it, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Abu Sufyan came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Muhammad, I adjure you by Allah and the ties of kinship, for we have eaten al-'ilhiz'—meaning fur with blood. Then Allah revealed: وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْناهُمْ بِالْعَذابِ فَمَا اسْتَكانُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ وَما يَتَضَرَّعُونَ."

I say: The narrations on this meaning are varied, and what we have cited is the most balanced. They point to a drought that occurred in Mecca and its surroundings due to the prayer of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the apparent sense of most of them is that it was after the Migration (الهجرة), but that does not agree with critical consideration.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوِ اتَّبَعَ الْحَقُّ أَهْواءَهُمْ, he said: "The Truth (الحق) is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام)."

I say: This is from the inner meaning (البطن) in the sense that preceded in the discussion of the clear and the ambiguous (المحكم والمتشابه). Similar to it is what he cited regarding His saying وَإِنَّكَ لَتَدْعُوهُمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ, he said: "To the guardianship (ولاية) of the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام)." Likewise, what he cited regarding His saying عَنِ الصِّراطِ لَناكِبُونَ, he said: "From the Imam they are deviating."

In it, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ خَرْجاً فَخَراجُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ, he says: "Or do you ask them for a wage? Then the wage of your Lord is better."

In al-Kafi, with his chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَمَا اسْتَكانُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ وَما يَتَضَرَّعُونَ. He said: 'Al-Istikanah is humility (الخضوع), and al-tadarru' (التضرع) is raising the hands and entreating with them.'"

In al-Majma', it is narrated from Muqatil ibn Hayyan from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Raising the hands is from al-istikanah.' I said: 'And what is al-istikanah?' He said: 'Do you not read this verse: فَمَا اسْتَكانُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ وَما يَتَضَرَّعُونَ?'" Al-Tha'labi and al-Wahidi cited it in their commentaries.

In it, Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Al-Istikanah is supplication (الدعاء), and al-tadarru' is raising the hands in prayer."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-'Askari narrated in al-Mawa'iz from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: فَمَا اسْتَكانُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ وَما يَتَضَرَّعُونَ: "That is, they did not humble themselves in supplication and did not submit—and had they submitted to Allah, He would have answered them."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا فَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ باباً ذا عَذابٍ شَدِيدٍ, Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "It is during the Return (الرجعة) — the belief that certain individuals will return to life before the Day of Resurrection."

Ayahs

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٨)
It is He who made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts. Little do you thank. (78)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى ذَرَأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٧٩)
It is He who created you on the earth, and you will be mustered toward Him. (79)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ وَلَهُ ٱخْتِلَـٰفُ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٨٠)
And it is He who gives life and brings death, and due to Him is the alternation of day and night. Do you not exercise your reason? (80)
بَلْ قَالُوا۟ مِثْلَ مَا قَالَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (٨١)
Indeed, they say, just like what the former peoples said. (81)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ (٨٢)
They said, ‘What, when we are dead and become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected? (82)
لَقَدْ وُعِدْنَا نَحْنُ وَءَابَآؤُنَا هَـٰذَا مِن قَبْلُ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٨٣)
Certainly, we and our fathers were promised this before. [But] these are nothing but myths of the ancients.’ (83)
قُل لِّمَنِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٨٤)
Say, ‘To whom does the earth belong and whoever it contains, if you know?’ (84)
سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٨٥)
They will say, ‘To Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you not then take admonition?’ (85)
قُلْ مَن رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ٱلسَّبْعِ وَرَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٨٦)
Say, ‘Who is the Lord of the seven heavens and the Lord of the Great Throne?’ (86)
سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٨٧)
They will say, ‘[They belong] to Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (87)
قُلْ مَنۢ بِيَدِهِۦ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلَا يُجَارُ عَلَيْهِ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٨٨)
Say, ‘In whose hand is the dominion of all things, and who gives shelter and no shelter can be provided from Him, if you know?’ (88)
سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ فَأَنَّىٰ تُسْحَرُونَ (٨٩)
They will say, ‘[They all belong] to Allah.’ Say, ‘Then how are you so deluded?’ (89)
بَلْ أَتَيْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (٩٠)
Indeed, We have brought them the truth, and they are indeed liars. (90)
مَا ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِن وَلَدٍ وَمَا كَانَ مَعَهُۥ مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ ۚ إِذًا لَّذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلَـٰهٍۭ بِمَا خَلَقَ وَلَعَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (٩١)
Allah has not taken any offspring, neither is there any god besides Him, for then each god would take away what he created, and some of them would surely rise up against others. Clear is Allah of what they allege! (91)
عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٩٢)
The Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is above having any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (92)
قُل رَّبِّ إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّى مَا يُوعَدُونَ (٩٣)
Say, ‘My Lord! If You should show me what they are promised, (93)
رَبِّ فَلَا تَجْعَلْنِى فِى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٩٤)
then do not put me, my Lord, among the wrongdoing lot.’ (94)
وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّرِيَكَ مَا نَعِدُهُمْ لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (٩٥)
We are indeed able to show you what We promise them. (95)
ٱدْفَعْ بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ ۚ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَصِفُونَ (٩٦)
Repel ill [conduct] with that which is the best. We know best whatever they allege. (96)
وَقُل رَّبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزَٰتِ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ (٩٧)
Say, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection from the promptings of devils, (97)
وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ رَبِّ أَن يَحْضُرُونِ (٩٨)
and I seek Your protection, my Lord, from their presence near me.’ (98)

(Commentary)
